Yet strikingly missing from this dating effort is the huge southern sill complex cropping out in the >0.6 × 10 6 km 2 Main Karoo sedimentary basin. We present 16 new 40 Ar/ 39 Ar analyses carried out on fresh plagioclase and biotite separates from 15 sill samples collected along a N–S trend in the eastern part of the basin. The results show a large range of plateau and miniplateau ages (176.2 ± 1.3 to 183.8 ± 2.4 Ma), with most dates suggesting a ∼3 Ma (181–184 Ma) duration for the main sill events. The available age database allows correlation of the Karoo LIP emplacement with the Pliensbachian‐Toarcian second‐order biotic extinction, the global warming, and the Toarcian anoxic event (provided that adequate calibration between the 40 K and 238 U decay constant is made). The mass extinction and the isotopic excursions recorded at the base of the Toarcian appear to be synchronous with both the increase of magma emission of the Karoo LIP and the emplacement of the sills. The CO 2 and SO 2 derived from both volcanic emissions as well as carbon‐rich sedimentary layers intruded by sills might be the main culprits of the Pliensbachian‐Toarcian climate perturbations. We propose that the relatively low eruption rate of the Karoo LIP is one of the main reasons explaining why its impact on the biosphere is relatively low contrary to, e.g., the CAMP (Triassic‐Jurassic) and Siberia (Permo‐Triassic) LIPs.

10.1038/s41598-022-08269-y.title: Rapid light carbon releases and increased aridity linked to Karoo–Ferrar magmatism during the early Toarcian oceanic anoxic event

authors.name: Céline Remazeilles
authors.institution: Laboratoire des Sciences de l'Ingénieur pour l'Environnement

authors.name: Ramón Egli
authors.institution: Central Institution for Meteorology and Geodynamics

10.5194/cp-14-303-2018.title: Synchronizing early Eocene deep-sea and continental records – cyclostratigraphic age models for the Bighorn Basin Coring Project drill cores
10.5194/cp-14-303-2018.abstract: Abstract. A consistent chronostratigraphic framework is required to understand the effect of major paleoclimate perturbations on both marine and terrestrial ecosystems. Transient global warming events in the early Eocene, at 56–54 Ma, show the impact of large-scale carbon input into the ocean–atmosphere system. Here we provide the first timescale synchronization of continental and marine deposits spanning the Paleocene–Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM) and the interval just prior to the Eocene Thermal Maximum 2 (ETM-2). Cyclic variations in geochemical data come from continental drill cores of the Bighorn Basin Coring Project (BBCP, Wyoming, USA) and from marine deep-sea drilling deposits retrieved by the Ocean Drilling Program (ODP). Both are dominated by eccentricity-modulated precession cycles used to construct a common cyclostratigraphic framework. Integration of age models results in a revised astrochronology for the PETM in deep-sea records that is now generally consistent with independent 3He age models. The duration of the PETM is estimated at ∼ 200 kyr for the carbon isotope excursion and ∼ 120 kyr for the associated pelagic clay layer. A common terrestrial and marine age model shows a concurrent major change in marine and terrestrial biota ∼ 200 kyr before ETM-2. In the Bighorn Basin, the change is referred to as Biohorizon B and represents a period of significant mammalian turnover and immigration, separating the upper Haplomylus–Ectocion Range Zone from the Bunophorus Interval Zone and approximating the Wa-4–Wa-5 land mammal zone boundary. In sediments from ODP Site 1262 (Walvis Ridge), major changes in the biota at this time are documented by the radiation of a “second generation” of apical spine-bearing sphenolith species (e.g., S. radians and S. editus), the emergence of T. orthostylus, and the marked decline of D. multiradiatus.

10.5194/cp-12-1151-2016.title: Environmental impact and magnitude of paleosol carbonate carbon isotope excursions marking five early Eocene hyperthermals in the Bighorn Basin, Wyoming
10.5194/cp-12-1151-2016.abstract: Abstract. Transient greenhouse warming events in the Paleocene and Eocene were associated with the addition of isotopically light carbon to the exogenic atmosphere–ocean carbon system, leading to substantial environmental and biotic change. The magnitude of an accompanying carbon isotope excursion (CIE) can be used to constrain both the sources and amounts of carbon released during an event and also to correlate marine and terrestrial records with high precision. The Paleocene–Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM) is well documented, but CIE records for the subsequent warming events are still rare, especially from the terrestrial realm.Here, we provide new paleosol carbonate CIE records for two of the smaller hyperthermal events, I1 and I2, as well as two additional records of Eocene Thermal Maximum 2 (ETM2) and H2 in the Bighorn Basin, Wyoming, USA. Stratigraphic comparison of this expanded, high-resolution terrestrial carbon isotope history to the deep-sea benthic foraminiferal isotope records from Ocean Drilling Program (ODP) sites 1262 and 1263, Walvis Ridge, in the southern Atlantic Ocean corroborates the idea that the Bighorn Basin fluvial sediments record global atmospheric change. The ∼ 34 m thicknesses of the eccentricity-driven hyperthermals in these archives corroborate precession forcing of the ∼ 7 m thick fluvial overbank–avulsion sedimentary cycles. Using bulk-oxide mean-annual-precipitation reconstructions, we find soil moisture contents during the four younger hyperthermals that are similar to or only slightly wetter than the background, in contrast with soil drying observed during the PETM using the same proxy, sediments, and plant fossils.The magnitude of the CIEs in soil carbonate for the four smaller, post-PETM events scale nearly linearly with the equivalent event magnitudes documented in marine records. In contrast, the magnitude of the PETM terrestrial CIE is at least 5 ‰ smaller than expected based on extrapolation of the scaling relationship established from the smaller events. We evaluate the potential for recently documented, nonlinear effects of pCO2 on plant photosynthetic C-isotope fractionation to explain this scaling discrepancy. We find that the PETM anomaly can be explained only if background pCO2 was at least 50 % lower during most of the post-PETM events than prior to the PETM. Although not inconsistent with other pCO2 proxy data for the time interval, this would require declining pCO2 across an interval of global warming. A more likely explanation of the PETM CIE anomaly in pedogenic carbonate is that other environmental or biogeochemical factors influencing the terrestrial CIE magnitudes were not similar in nature or proportional to event size across all of the hyperthermals. We suggest that contrasting regional hydroclimatic change between the PETM and subsequent events, in line with our soil proxy records, may have modulated the expression of the global CIEs in the Bighorn Basin soil carbonate records.

10.1073/pnas.0800885105.title: Ocean methane hydrates as a slow tipping point in the global carbon cycle
10.1073/pnas.0800885105.venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
10.1073/pnas.0800885105.abstract: We present a model of the global methane inventory as hydrate and bubbles below the sea floor. The model predicts the inventory of CH 4 in the ocean today to be ≈1600–2,000 Pg of C. Most of the hydrate in the model is in the Pacific, in large part because lower oxygen levels enhance the preservation of organic carbon. Because the oxygen concentration today may be different from the long-term average, the sensitivity of the model to O 2 is a source of uncertainty in predicting hydrate inventories. Cold water column temperatures in the high latitudes lead to buildup of hydrates in the Arctic and Antarctic at shallower depths than is possible in low latitudes. A critical bubble volume fraction threshold has been proposed as a critical threshold at which gas migrates all through the sediment column. Our model lacks many factors that lead to heterogeneity in the real hydrate reservoir in the ocean, such as preferential hydrate formation in sandy sediments and subsurface gas migration, and is therefore conservative in its prediction of releasable methane, finding only 35 Pg of C released after 3 °C of uniform warming by using a 10% critical bubble volume. If 2.5% bubble volume is taken as critical, then 940 Pg of C might escape in response to 3 °C warming. This hydrate model embedded into a global climate model predicts ≈0.4–0.5 °C additional warming from the hydrate response to fossil fuel CO 2 release, initially because of methane, but persisting through the 10-kyr duration of the simulations because of the CO 2 oxidation product of methane.

10.1016/j.epsl.2018.11.029.title: Terrestrial sources as the primary delivery mechanism of mercury to the oceans across the Toarcian Oceanic Anoxic Event (Early Jurassic)

10.1016/j.epsl.2016.11.021.title: High-resolution carbon isotope records of the Toarcian Oceanic Anoxic Event (Early Jurassic) from North America and implications for the global drivers of the Toarcian carbon cycle
10.1016/j.epsl.2016.11.021.abstract: The Mesozoic Era experienced several instances of abrupt environmental change that are associated with instabilities in the climate, reorganizations of the global carbon cycle, and elevated extinction rates. Often during these perturbations, oxygen-deficient conditions developed in the oceans resulting in the widespread deposition of organic-rich sediments — these events are referred to as Oceanic Anoxic Events or OAEs. Such events have been linked to massive injections of greenhouse gases into the ocean–atmosphere system by transient episodes of voluminous volcanism and the destabilization of methane clathrates within marine environments. Nevertheless, uncertainty surrounds the specific environmental drivers and feedbacks that occurred during the OAEs that caused perturbations in the carbon cycle; this is particularly true of the Early Jurassic Toarcian OAE (∼183.1 Ma). Here, we present biostratigraphically constrained carbon isotope data from western North America (Alberta and British Columbia, Canada) to better assess the global extent of the carbon cycle perturbations. We identify the large negative carbon isotope excursion associated with the OAE along with high-frequency oscillations and steps within the onset of this excursion. We propose that these high-frequency carbon isotope excursions reflect changes to the global carbon cycle and also that they are related to the production and release of greenhouse gases from terrestrial environments on astronomical timescales. Furthermore, increased terrestrial methanogenesis should be considered an important climatic feedback during Ocean Anoxic Events and other similar events in Earth history after the proliferation of land plants.

10.1016/j.gr.2017.12.002.title: Environmental changes during the Cretaceous-Paleogene mass extinction and Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum: Implications for the Anthropocene

10.5194/cp-19-1623-2023.title: Tracing North Atlantic volcanism and seaway connectivity across the Paleocene–Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM)
key_findings: Peak North Atlantic Igneous Province (NAIP) activity was precisely constrained to 56.0-55.8 million years ago, a 200,000-year window occurring during the PETM carbon isotope excursion body, establishing tight temporal correlation with the thermal maximum

The North Sea underwent rapid isolation from the North Atlantic within approximately 11,900 years during PETM recovery, evidenced by extreme radiogenic shifts in osmium isotopes (0.3 to 0.83), caused by regional uplift from magma plume dynamics

Mercury accumulation rates during the PETM body averaged 1.76 nanograms per square centimeter per year—an order of magnitude higher than PETM onset—indicating sustained elevated volcanic degassing despite sparse visible ash layers in lower and middle PETM sections

Enhanced silicate weathering of fresh basaltic material, documented through smectite/zeolite minerals and negative lithium isotope excursions, created a significant carbon sink that likely contributed to PETM termination through atmospheric CO₂ sequestration

Post-PETM ash layer abundance reflects a shift from effusive to explosive volcanic activity driven by magma-water interactions in shallow environments rather than increased overall activity, demonstrating that ash layer frequency is not a direct proxy for volcanism magnitude
10.5194/cp-19-1623-2023.methodology: Multiple complementary geochemical and paleontological proxies analyzed across the exceptionally well-preserved Fur Formation succession in Denmark, including volcanic ash layer chronostratigraphy, mercury/total organic carbon ratios, lithium and osmium isotope geochemistry, and lipid biomarker paleothermometry spanning the complete pre-PETM to early Eocene interval.
limitations: The study is regionally focused on the North Atlantic/North Sea region; broader applicability requires confirmation from other expanded PETM successions globally
Precision of osmium isotope records is limited by diagenetic alteration and the potential for post-depositional open-system behavior in clay-rich sediments
Paleotemperature estimates from TEX₈₆ are complicated by basin restriction and salinity changes, making interpretation ambiguous in semi-enclosed settings
Carbon source volumes and isotopic compositions remain incompletely constrained despite tight chronological framework for NAIP activity
Organic carbon burial rate changes during the PETM body remain incompletely resolved, limiting quantitative carbon cycle mass balance calculations
future_work: Expand proxy records to additional expanded PETM successions globally to test regional North Atlantic findings on a global scale and identify potential basin-specific versus global patterns
Develop higher-resolution constraints on organic carbon burial rate variations throughout the PETM using multiple independent methods to resolve carbon cycle paradoxes
Investigate mechanisms and timescales of seaway connectivity changes in other restricted basins during the PETM to assess global oceanographic reorganization patterns
Model coupled effects of NAIP volatile degassing, contact metamorphism, and weathering feedbacks using updated carbon source parameters to test climate sensitivity assumptions
Integrate detailed eruptive style analysis (phreatomagmatic versus subaerial) with paleoenvironmental records to better constrain atmospheric versus submarine volcanic degassing contributions
themes: Ocean gateway evolution and seaway connectivity
Biogeochemical weathering feedbacks and carbon cycling

10.1038/s41467-022-33390-x.title: Astrochronology of the Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum on the Atlantic Coastal Plain
key_findings: The PETM carbon isotope excursion onset lasted approximately 6 kyr, resolving a long-standing debate between estimates ranging from 13 years to 20 kyr by using rigorous astrochronological methods with statistical hypothesis testing

Astronomical forcing was demonstrably involved in the PETM, with the onset occurring at an extreme in precession during a maximum in eccentricity cycles, indicating orbital modulation may have triggered the event

High-resolution time series analysis of calcium content and magnetic susceptibility from the Howards Tract cores shows statistically significant (>98.6% confidence) cyclic variations paced by 20 kyr precession cycles and 100 kyr short eccentricity cycles

Carbonate saturation 'overshoot' occurred during the PETM recovery phase on the Atlantic paleo-shelf, with CaCO₃ content increasing from 1.9% during the PETM body to 9.9% in the post-PETM Nanjemoy Formation, demonstrating basin-wide ocean acidification followed by alkalinity recovery

cGENIE Earth system modeling indicates that astronomically forced variations in insolation control carbonate productivity through effects on surface temperature, weathering rates, and ocean carbonate ion concentration
10.1038/s41467-022-33390-x.methodology: Astrochronological analysis of two spliced cores from Howards Tract, Maryland using high-resolution XRF scanning (5 mm) and magnetic susceptibility measurements combined with multiple statistical methods (COCO, ASM, TimeOpt, evolutionary FFT, wavelet analysis). Results were validated using the cGENIE Earth system model with transient astronomical forcing parameters from the La2004 orbital solution.
limitations: Uncertainty in defining the precise top of the CIE onset due to variable sampling resolution, particularly in the carbonate-depleted interval
Assumption of constant sedimentation rate within individual precession cycles, which may overestimate onset duration if rates increased significantly at the Aquia-Marlboro contact

Early diagenetic precipitation of siderite with anomalously low δ¹³C amplified the measured carbon isotope excursion (~13‰) relative to the global signal (~4-5‰), making the onset appear potentially more abrupt than the original signal
Core record truncated by unconformity at the top of Marlboro Clay, preserving only the lowermost ~110 kyr of the PETM event rather than the complete interval
Spectral methods may be complicated by autogenic sedimentation processes in coastal/shelf environments, including storms, tides, bioturbation, and variable sedimentation rates
future_work: Compare astrochronological results from additional high-sedimentation-rate coastal/shelf sections globally to independently verify the 6 kyr onset duration
Develop improved methods to deconvolve early diagenetic versus primary oceanographic signals in carbonate isotope records from mobile mud belt environments
Integrate detailed benthic and planktonic foraminifera records with astrochronology to better constrain carbon isotope excursion dynamics
Conduct transient modeling of methane hydrate destabilization and permafrost carbon release triggered by orbital forcing to test mechanism linkages
Investigate whether similar orbital configurations (precession extrema during eccentricity maxima) at other early Eocene thermal events support a general orbital trigger hypothesis

10.1029/2019pa003556.title: A High‐Fidelity Benthic Stable Isotope Record of Late Cretaceous–Early Eocene Climate Change and Carbon‐Cycling
key_findings: 405-kyr and 100-kyr eccentricity cycles paced climate and carbon cycle changes during the Late Maastrichtian-Early Eocene (~67-52 Ma), with the carbon cycle lagging climate by ~23,000 years at the 405-kyr band and ~3,000-4,500 years at the 100-kyr band

Hyperthermal events (rapid warming episodes) were triggered by orbital forcing and amplified by positive feedback from release of isotopically light carbon from temperature-sensitive reservoirs such as methane hydrates, permafrost, and peat deposits
The Dan-C2 event in the Early Paleocene does not represent a global hyperthermal despite orbital pacing, as it lacks evidence for significant deep-ocean warming in benthic oxygen isotope records

Large Igneous Province (LIP) volcanism from the Deccan Traps and North Atlantic Igneous Province elevated atmospheric pCO₂ (reaching ~1,000+ ppm) and increased climate sensitivity to orbital forcing during warmer intervals
Atlantic-Pacific deep-water carbon isotope gradients were disrupted following the K/Pg mass extinction and the PETM, suggesting periodic decoupling of deep-water formation between ocean basins
10.1029/2019pa003556.methodology: Paired stable carbon and oxygen isotope analysis of benthic foraminifera (Nuttalides truempy) from ODP Site 1262 (South Atlantic) spanning 14.75 million years (~67.1-52.35 Ma). Records were orbitally tuned using the 405-kyr eccentricity cycle and calibrated to the La2010b astronomical solution, then subjected to spectral analysis and cross-spectral phasing analysis to identify orbital forcing mechanisms and time-lag relationships between climate and carbon cycle changes.
limitations: Paleocene atmospheric pCO₂ data are scarce with considerable scatter, making quantitative constraints on CO₂ levels during this interval uncertain
The Dan-C2 event may represent regional rather than global climate perturbation confined to Atlantic and Tethyan surface ocean but not affecting deep-water formation regions
Elemental Fe intensity and sedimentation rate patterns may be influenced by biogenic carbonate productivity changes and eolian dust flux rather than solely by carbonate dissolution
Interpretation of longer-term cycles (>1 million years) is complicated by potential distorting effects of pre-spectral analysis detrending on the data
The study relies on a single marine site; while comparison to Pacific records confirms global synchronicity, regional-scale variations may not be fully captured
future_work: Investigate whether temperature- and climate-sensitive carbon reservoirs (methane hydrates, permafrost, peat) became more unstable during warmer climates and how this sensitivity may apply to future anthropogenic warming
Examine potential contributions of low-latitude biogenic CO₂ sources and changes in thermohaline circulation intensity to explain Atlantic-Pacific deep-water mass decoupling episodes
Develop higher-resolution records from multiple ocean basins to better constrain regional versus global expressions of Early Paleocene climate perturbations like the Dan-C2 event
Assess the relative importance of the 2.4 million-year eccentricity modulation on hyperthermal frequency during different climate states and volcanic activity intervals
Model the combined effects of LIP volcanism and orbital forcing on climate and carbon cycle sensitivity to better predict future climate responses
themes: Paleogene climate change and greenhouse world
Carbon cycle dynamics and isotope geochemistry
Large Igneous Province volcanism and climate feedbacks

10.1038/nature06588.title: An early Cenozoic perspective on greenhouse warming and carbon-cycle dynamics
key_findings: Equilibrium climate sensitivity is approximately 1.2 ± 0.3°C per W/m² of radiative forcing (roughly 4.8 ± 1.2°C for doubled CO₂), based on paleoclimate data from the Last Glacial Maximum-Holocene and Prior Glacial Maximum-Eemian transitions, contradicting IPCC estimates that permit values as low as 1.5°C for doubled CO₂

Current greenhouse gas forcing of 4.6 W/m² would produce approximately 8-10°C of equilibrium warming when including ice sheet feedbacks and accounting for aerosol cooling, with only ~1.2°C realized to date, leaving 6.8-8.8°C of warming 'in the pipeline' even if atmospheric composition were frozen

The Cenozoic paleoclimate record reveals gradual cooling driven by declining CO₂ from plate tectonics, with current CO₂ levels (>420 ppm) exceeding those of Earth's most recent naturally warm periods, and Eocene CO₂ levels reconstructed at ~1200 ppm

International Maritime Organization shipping fuel regulations produced a dramatic +1.05 W/m² increase in absorbed solar radiation after 2015, providing observational evidence that aerosol forcing is more negative than IPCC estimates (~-1.1 W/m²) and currently masking 2°C of warming from greenhouse gases

Earth's energy imbalance declines much faster than surface temperature responds, with rapid ultrafast cloud feedbacks operating on timescales of years rather than decades, implying that observed energy imbalance understates the forcing reduction needed for climate stabilization
10.1038/nature06588.methodology: The authors employ a paleoclimate-based approach comparing stable climate states separated by known radiative forcing changes (Last Glacial Maximum to Holocene, and Prior Glacial Maximum to Eemian) to empirically constrain climate sensitivity while avoiding model dependencies. They combine paleoclimate proxy analysis (benthic oxygen isotopes, noble gas solubility) with climate modeling and contemporary satellite observations to infer climate parameters and project future warming scenarios.
limitations: The paper acknowledges that climate sensitivity exhibits state dependence, particularly at very cold temperatures approaching snowball Earth conditions, though state dependence is modest for the climatologically relevant range
Ocean circulation and ice sheet dynamics are explicitly set aside for treatment in a companion paper, limiting the scope of mechanisms analyzed
Definitively attributing the IMO 2020 shipping experiment's radiative forcing change requires more detailed aerosol and cloud measurements
Direct proxy measurements of Cenozoic CO₂ show large scatter, so the inferred CO₂ history from temperature inversion may have substantial uncertainties

Uncertainty in historical aerosol forcing creates ambiguity in 20th-century warming attribution, with multiple combinations of climate sensitivity and aerosol forcing potentially matching observations
future_work: Development of more detailed aerosol and cloud measurements to definitively constrain aerosol forcing and validate findings from the IMO 2020 shipping regulations natural experiment
Further research into solar radiation management technologies, including marine cloud brightening via sea salt aerosols, as potential tools for rapid climate stabilization
Enhanced characterization of ultrafast cloud feedbacks operating on sub-decadal timescales and their physical mechanisms
Improved ice sheet models to capture feedback mechanisms that may lower the CO₂ threshold for Antarctic deglaciation below current 700-840 ppm estimates
Investigation of temporal feedbacks and energy imbalance dynamics to refine projections of committed warming and climate response timescales
themes: Paleoclimate reconstruction and Cenozoic climate history
Aerosol forcing and Earth's energy balance
Long-term carbon cycle and plate tectonics

10.1126/science.aba6853.title: An astronomically dated record of Earth’s climate and its predictability over the last 66 million years
key_findings: An astronomically dated climate record spanning 66 million years reveals distinct climate modes (Warm and Cool Modes) with ~150 million-year cyclicity throughout the Phanerozoic, suggesting fundamental periodic organization of Earth's climate system

Orbital forcing mechanisms (Milankovitch cycles at 23, 41, and 100 thousand-year periods) drive glacial-interglacial oscillations in the Quaternary, with atmospheric CO₂ variations amplifying orbital perturbations by 40-90 ppm during glacial-interglacial transitions

Carbon cycle dynamics, particularly the 'reverse pump' mechanism releasing stored carbon through volcanism, organic matter oxidation, and metamorphism, control major climate transitions between modes, with δ¹³C shifts of 1.5-3‰ indicating rapid atmospheric CO₂ changes

Phanerozoic climate exhibits no directional trend toward overall warming or cooling over 570 million years; instead, the system demonstrates threshold-like behavior with extended stable periods punctuated by abrupt transitions between climate modes

Southern Ocean processes, particularly Antarctic temperature changes and sea-ice extent variations, lead Northern Hemisphere climate responses and serve as the pacemaker for global climate oscillations at multiple timescales
10.1126/science.aba6853.methodology: The authors synthesized paleoclimate data from multiple sources including sedimentological proxies (evaporites, glacial deposits, coals), paleontological indicators (faunal/floral distributions, isotopic compositions of fossils), and geochemical evidence (oxygen and carbon isotope records, ice-core CO₂ measurements). Orbital forcing analysis employed spectral methods on paleoclimate records, while climate modeling with prognostic equations for ice sheets, ocean circulation, and atmospheric composition was used to test mechanistic hypotheses.
limitations: Paleoclimate reconstructions depend on disparate data sources with inconsistent chronological timescales, creating persistent correlation difficulties even with improved dating methods
Oxygen isotope paleothermometry is complicated by diagenetic alteration, uncertain salinity effects, and unknown environmental tolerances of extinct organisms

The 'reverse pump' mechanism explaining rapid carbon isotope shifts during climate transitions is 'almost entirely unknown and unspecified,' representing a significant gap in understanding climate forcing
Incomplete stratigraphic sequences with erosional gaps limit ability to precisely identify triggering mechanisms for major climate mode transitions

Temperature and humidity paleoclimate estimates originate from diverse analytical approaches applied to different rock and fossil types, creating considerable quantitative uncertainties in specific paleoclimate reconstructions
future_work: Develop more sophisticated biogeochemical models to explain the mechanisms of the 'reverse pump' and rapid δ¹³C shifts observed during climate transitions, particularly distinguishing contributions from volcanism, metamorphism, and organic matter oxidation
Conduct higher-resolution paleoclimate analysis to detect previously unrecognized climate variability within Warm Modes, potentially revealing additional oscillations at shorter and longer timescales
Investigate mechanisms linking tectonic processes (ocean gateway opening/closure, orogeny, volcanism) to carbon cycle dynamics and atmospheric CO₂ variations over geological timescales
Test whether observed ~150 million-year climate mode cyclicity relates to galactic-scale orbital mechanics (half-Galactic Year) through integrated astronomical and paleoclimate modeling
Improve precision of paleoclimate dating and correlation to better constrain timing relationships between carbon isotope shifts, temperature changes, and sea-level variations during mode transitions
themes: Carbon cycle dynamics and atmospheric CO₂ forcing
Tectonic controls on long-term climate evolution

10.1038/nature17423.title: Changing atmospheric CO2 concentration was the primary driver of early Cenozoic climate
key_findings: Changing atmospheric CO2 concentration was a primary driver of early Cenozoic climate change, demonstrated through boron isotope proxy analysis of foraminiferal shells
Climate models significantly overestimate contemporary temperature trends relative to satellite and weather balloon measurements, with systematic warm bias in surface temperature records

Multiple lines of evidence indicate natural variability from ocean oscillations (PDO, AMO, ENSO) and solar forcing mechanisms explain most observed 20th-21st century temperature variations independently of CO2 forcing
Temperature records contain significant artifacts from station dropout, poor siting, data interpolation, and questionable homogenization procedures that artificially inflate reported warming trends

Paleoclimate evidence spanning 1000+ years demonstrates recurring 25-30 year climate cycles synchronized with solar activity variations, indicating natural climate oscillations independent of anthropogenic forcing
10.1038/nature17423.methodology: The paper employs multiple methodological approaches including: boron isotope geochemistry analysis of foraminiferal calcite shells to reconstruct ancient atmospheric pCO2 levels; statistical quality control charting (I-M/ReR/S control charts) applied to instrumental temperature records to identify discontinuities; spectral analysis of paleoclimate proxies (ice cores, tree rings, corals) spanning millennial timescales; linear system theory and Fourier analysis to characterize transfer functions between solar forcing and surface temperature responses; empirical climate sensitivity calculations using observational radiosonde data and satellite measurements; comparative analysis of competing climate models against instrumental records.
limitations: Paleoclimate reconstructions depend on indirect proxies (oxygen isotopes, boron isotopes) with inherent measurement uncertainties and assumptions about past ocean chemistry
Satellite temperature measurements (UAH, RSS) are available only since 1979, limiting temporal perspective for validating long-term climate hypotheses

Surface weather station network has degraded substantially (from ~6000 to <1500 stations), with non-random station dropout concentrated in cold regions, limiting spatial coverage and introducing systematic warm bias
Alternative climate sensitivity models presented lack independent empirical verification—predictions remain testable but unfalsified
Ocean circulation and multidecadal oscillation mechanisms are incompletely understood; attribution of specific temperature changes to PDO/AMO versus anthropogenic forcing remains contested
Solar amplification mechanisms (cosmic ray-cloud coupling, UV-ozone effects) are controversial and rejected by mainstream climate science; their magnitude remains uncertain
The paper's extensive critique of IPCC methodology and consensus science, while methodologically rigorous, reflects editorial selection bias toward skeptical perspectives
future_work: Establish continuous Earth energy balance monitoring through proposed Lunar Observatory to directly measure outgoing radiation and planetary albedo in multiple spectral bands
Develop improved solar forcing models incorporating multiple amplification mechanisms (UV-ozone chemistry, cosmic ray effects) with quantified parameter uncertainty
Reconstruct millennial-scale climate variations using multiple independent paleoclimate archives to better characterize natural oscillation magnitudes and periodicities
Conduct rigorous hindcast testing of competing climate models against well-characterized paleoclimate intervals including Medieval Warm Period, Little Ice Age, and Holocene thermal optimum
Analyze mechanisms driving the 1945-1977 cooling interval during rapid CO2 emissions to disambiguate natural versus anthropogenic forcing contributions
Examine why water vapor emission layer has descended rather than ascended despite surface warming and increased atmospheric water vapor content
Investigate reasons for systematic divergence between surface-based temperature records and satellite-based tropospheric measurements since 1979
Test predictions of alternative solar-forced climate model by monitoring for significant cooling (≥0.1°C) commencing 2020-2030 as solar activity enters Grand Minimum phase

10.1016/j.earscirev.2013.07.004.title: Climate model and proxy data constraints on ocean warming across the Paleocene–Eocene Thermal Maximum
key_findings: A cross-interface model (CIM) is proposed to accurately describe two-dimensional thermal transport at cross-interfaces between overlapped nanoribbons, moving beyond previous one-dimensional approximations
The dimensionless factor η (η < 1) plays a critical role in controlling thermal resistance at cross-interfaces, with total thermal resistance expressed as R_total = R_intra + (1/η) × R_inter
MD simulations of boron nitride nanoribbons validated the CIM with high coefficient of determination (R² > 0.998), confirming the model's accuracy across different vacancy concentrations

Thermal transport at cross-interfaces can be improved by decreasing the ratio of in-plane to inter-ribbon thermal resistance (λ₁) or reducing the relative difference between in-plane thermal resistances of two ribbons (λ₂)

Vacancy defects in nanoribbons significantly degrade thermal transport by increasing both in-plane thermal resistance and inter-ribbon thermal resistance, with the effect primarily driven by changes in λ₁ rather than λ₂
10.1016/j.earscirev.2013.07.004.methodology: An analytical cross-interface model (CIM) was derived based on Fourier's law and energy conservation equations for two overlapped nanoribbons with different thermal properties. The model was validated through non-equilibrium molecular dynamics (NEMD) simulations of boron nitride nanoribbons, with various vacancy concentrations and configurations tested to explore parameter influences.
limitations: CIM assumes constant thermal properties within ribbons and may not be exact for complex systems with many unaligned stacked ribbons or inhomogeneous materials with centralized defects

The analytical model does not directly account for system-size dependent effects on thermal conductivity at the nanoscale, though this limitation is discussed regarding how in-plane thermal conductivity increases with ribbon length
Simulations were performed with fixed ribbon lengths, limiting the exploration of finite-size effects on thermal transport properties
future_work: Extend CIM to handle complex systems with multiple unaligned stacked ribbons and inhomogeneous materials
Develop multi-scale modeling combining CIM with finite element simulations to study thermal transport in more realistic composite structures
Investigate other defect types beyond vacancies (such as covalent bonds and isotope effects) to further optimize thermal transport at cross-interfaces
Apply CIM to predict and optimize thermal management in practical electronic devices using low-dimensional materials as thermal interface materials
themes: Analytical modeling and molecular dynamics simulations

10.1029/2023pa004633.title: Astronomical Pacing of Middle Eocene Sea‐Level Fluctuations: Inferences From Shallow‐Water Carbonate Ramp Deposits
10.1029/2023pa004633.short_summary: This study analyzes middle Eocene shallow-water carbonate deposits in the Paris Basin to reconstruct sea-level fluctuations. The research finds that third-order sea-level changes (~5–10 m amplitude) were paced by 2.4-million-year very long eccentricity cycles, suggesting periodic polar ice build-up during low eccentricity nodes. A key exception occurred during the Middle Eocene Climatic Optimum (MECO), where a rapid sea-level rise of ~30 m decoupled from astronomical forcing, likely driven by increased atmospheric CO₂. The work provides direct geological evidence that middle Eocene sea-level variability was largely insolation-driven but could be overridden during abrupt warming events.
key_findings: Elementary sequences in Middle Eocene Paris Basin deposits correspond to the 0.405 Myr orbital eccentricity cycle, with third-order depositional sequences paced by the ~2.4 Myr very long eccentricity cycle
Third-order eustatic sea-level fluctuations during the Lutetian were small, estimated at 5-10 meters, driven by insolation-forced changes in polar ice volume

During the Middle Eocene Climatic Optimum (MECO, ~40.3 Ma), a dramatic sea-level rise of ~30 meters or more occurred, independent of astronomical pacing and likely driven by increased atmospheric CO₂ from volcanism
Shoreline retreat rates during the MECO were ~500 km/Myr, approximately 10 times faster than typical Lutetian third-order cycles (~50 km/Myr)
Sea-level lowstands correspond to nodes of the very long eccentricity cycle (at 40.25, 43.1, 45.1, and 48 Ma), where reduced insolation variations facilitated polar ice accumulation
10.1029/2023pa004633.methodology: Sequence stratigraphic analysis of shallow-water mixed carbonate-clastic deposits in the Paris Basin, France, calibrated astronomically using biostratigraphic data (calcareous nannoplankton, dinoflagellates, benthic foraminifera) and correlated with orbital solutions (La2011). Fischer Plot methodology was applied with decompaction/compaction corrections to quantify eustatic sea-level changes from sedimentary accommodation space patterns.
limitations: Limited biostratigraphic resolution in shallow-water deposits hampers demonstration of global synchronization
Thickness data for individual elementary sequences in the Calcaire Grossier Supérieur (L2, L3) not systematically available; thicknesses had to be modeled
The 22 elementary sequences identified may contain undetected hiatuses that would affect age model precision
Maximum offshore flooding rates during the MECO are minimum estimates due to limited resolution of the 0.405 Myr age model
Fischer Plot methodology does not account for compaction effects or quantitatively reconstruct accommodation space evolution without additional corrections
Uncertain stratigraphic position of the Falun de Foulangues marker bed within the CGS complicates sequence boundary placement
future_work: Direct astronomical calibration of other Middle Eocene shallow-water carbonate platforms and ramps to test global applicability of observed sea-level pacing patterns
Quantitative examination of skeletal preservation and calcification patterns in Paris Basin fossils during the MECO to assess impacts of ocean acidification on epicontinental shelf ecosystems
Constraint of atmospheric CO₂ levels during the MECO through silicate weathering feedback dynamics recorded in calcification patterns
Investigation of the decoupling mechanism between insolation forcing and sea-level changes during the MECO transient warming event
Comparison of early Bartonian sea-level record with updated paleoceanographic models to better understand MECO climate forcing and greenhouse gas dynamics
themes: Middle Eocene sea-level change and glacioeustasy
Orbital eccentricity cycles and climate pacing

10.5194/cp-2023-70.title: Polar amplification of orbital-scale climate variability in the early Eocene greenhouse world
10.5194/cp-2023-70.short_summary: This study reconstructs early Eocene tropical sea surface temperatures using high-resolution biomarker data from the equatorial Atlantic. The results show synchronous warming across both tropical and high latitudes during orbitally-paced carbon cycle perturbations, confirming these as global hyperthermal events. Critically, the analysis reveals that temperature variability in the deep ocean (a proxy for high latitudes) was amplified by a factor of 1.7–2.3 compared to the tropics. This ice-free polar amplification, driven by carbon cycle and hydrological feedbacks, suggests climate models may underestimate non-ice related feedbacks and that such processes could influence orbital-scale climate variability across geological time.
key_findings: Deep-ocean temperature variability during early Eocene hyperthermals was amplified by a factor of 1.7–2.3 compared to tropical surface ocean, demonstrating non-ice-related polar amplification in an ice-free greenhouse world

Six orbitally-triggered carbon cycle perturbations (events I1, I2, J, K, L1, L2) represent genuine transient global warming events, proving that orbital forcing drove atmospheric pCO₂ variability through carbon cycle feedbacks during the early Eocene
Fully-coupled climate models underestimate polar amplification by 20–50%, suggesting that hydrological and non-ice-albedo feedbacks are underrepresented in current IPCC-class models

Global mean sea surface temperature varied by up to 0.7°C on ~100-kyr eccentricity timescales outside declared hyperthermals, requiring substantial atmospheric pCO₂ variations (~118 ppm) and sensitive carbon cycle feedbacks

Polar amplification factors remain consistent across both orbital (10⁴-year) and long-term (10⁶-year) timescales in the ice-free Eocene, indicating that feedback mechanisms controlling meridional temperature gradients operate on multiple timescales
10.5194/cp-2023-70.methodology: High-resolution (~4 kyr) biomarker-based sea surface temperature reconstructions using TEX₈₆ from ODP Site 959 in the tropical Atlantic were compared to benthic foraminiferal δ¹⁸O-based deep-ocean bottom water temperatures from the CENOGRID compilation. Multiple stratigraphic correlation methods (standard deviation analysis, carbon isotope correlation, and paleothermometry refinement) were employed to robustly quantify polar amplification on orbital timescales.
limitations: Single tropical site (ODP 959) used for tropical endmember; ideally multiple sites should be averaged across complete latitudinal band
Authigenic carbonate component compromises absolute values of bulk carbonate δ¹³C and δ¹⁸O, limiting reconstruction of precise surface ocean dissolved inorganic carbon variations

Some correlations between tropical SST and deep-ocean records were finetuned using paleothermometry data, which could introduce circular reasoning or erroneous correlations of local temperature variations
High-resolution tropical temperature records for post-PETM hyperthermals remain sparse, limiting validation of results to early Eocene interval (~54–52 Ma)
Paleoclimate record cannot approach current rates of anthropogenic climate change, limiting direct applicability to future projections
Uncertainties in TEX₈₆ calibration (using both SST and subsurface temperature calibrations creates ~0.6 °C range in reconstructed amplification factors)
future_work: Develop additional high-resolution tropical SST records from multiple sites to provide robust latitudinal coverage and validate single-site findings
Investigate the specific carbon cycle feedback mechanisms responsible for orbital-scale climate variability in ice-free climates and their potential role in Pleistocene glacial-interglacial cycles
Improve climate model representation of non-ice-albedo hydrological feedbacks to better capture polar amplification in greenhouse climates
Examine whether carbon cycle feedbacks independent of ice, snow, and frost processes operated throughout Phanerozoic time and under different climate states
Refine understanding of how different timescale feedback mechanisms (acting on 10³–10⁴ year versus 10⁶ year scales) interact to control meridional temperature gradients
Develop improved TEX₈₆ calibrations for warm Eocene climates to reduce uncertainty in tropical temperature reconstructions

10.1038/nature03814.title: Astronomical pacing of late Palaeocene to early Eocene global warming events
10.1038/nature03814.short_summary: This study identifies a second early Eocene global warming event, termed the Elmo horizon (or ETM2), approximately 2 million years after the Palaeocene–Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM). Through orbital tuning of deep-sea sediment cores, the authors demonstrate that both hyperthermal events coincide with maxima in ~405-kyr and ~100-kyr eccentricity cycles, following a prolonged minimum in a ~2.25-Myr cycle. This astronomical pacing challenges previous interpretations that excluded orbital forcing for the PETM. The findings suggest that extreme seasonal contrasts during eccentricity maxima could have repeatedly triggered oceanic methane hydrate dissociation, providing a unified mechanism for these pronounced climatic perturbations.
key_findings: A second global warming event (ETM2/Elmo horizon) occurred ~2 million years after the Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM), characterized by similar geochemical and biotic features but approximately half the magnitude

Both the PETM and ETM2 correspond to maxima in the ~405-kyr and ~100-kyr eccentricity cycles that follow prolonged minima in the ~2.25-Myr eccentricity cycle, demonstrating they are astronomically paced
The Elmo horizon shows a negative carbon isotope excursion of 1.4-1.6‰ and a ~1.6‰ oxygen isotope excursion, with sea surface temperature rise of 3-4°C, approximately half that of the PETM

High-resolution spectral analysis of color reflectance and magnetic susceptibility revealed clear ~405-kyr and ~100-kyr eccentricity cycles in the sedimentary record, enabling orbital tuning of early Eocene sediments

Methane hydrate dissociation was likely triggered during eccentricity maxima when extreme seasonal contrasts increased intermediate seawater temperatures, explaining both thermal events through a common mechanism
10.1038/nature03814.methodology: Ocean Drilling Program Leg 208 cores from the Walvis Ridge were analyzed using high-resolution stable isotope analysis (bulk and individual foraminifera specimens), magnetic susceptibility measurements, and spectral analysis of color reflectance to extract orbital cycles. Orbital tuning was performed by correlating extracted eccentricity cycles to astronomical computations.
limitations: Absolute age uncertainty of ~20 kyr at 50 Myr ago limits precision of orbital solution
Chaotic behavior of inner planets creates large uncertainty in determining timing of ~2.25-Myr eccentricity cycle minima
Only 'floating tuning' could be achieved due to limited precision of orbital solutions older than 45 Myr
Bioturbation and post-depositional processes affected isotopic signals, particularly in benthic foraminifera records
The Elmo horizon has not been definitively recognized at other marine locations, limiting global correlation
future_work: Search for and characterize additional hyperthermal events in early Paleogene records to establish global patterns
Improve orbital tuning methodology and resolve uncertainties in long-term (~2.25-Myr) eccentricity cycle timing
Investigate methane hydrate reservoir dynamics and conditions necessary for dissociation during eccentricity maxima
Study mechanisms controlling the decreasing amplitude of successive thermal events (ETM2, H1, H2) during the early Eocene climatic optimum
Correlate additional records globally to better constrain the Elmo horizon and establish more precise chronological framework

10.5194/cp-5-309-2009.title: High resolution cyclostratigraphy of the early Eocene – new insights into the origin of the Cenozoic cooling trend
10.5194/cp-5-309-2009.short_summary: This study presents a high-resolution cyclostratigraphy from ODP Site 1258 in the tropical Atlantic, revising the durations of early Eocene magnetochrons (C21r to C24n) and offering a new calibration for the Geomagnetic Polarity Time Scale. A key finding is an ∼800-kyr interval within Chron C22r where typically dominant eccentricity-modulated precession cycles are replaced by strong obliquity cycles, even at this low-latitude site. The authors interpret this unique orbital configuration—coinciding with a proposed drop in atmospheric CO₂ below a critical threshold—as evidence for high-latitude climate forcing and the potential formation of the first ephemeral Antarctic ice sheets around 50 million years ago, marking the onset of the Cenozoic cooling trend.
key_findings: High-resolution cyclostratigraphy of early Eocene sediments from ODP Site 1258 reveals revised durations for magnetochrons C21r through C24n, with C24n estimated at 1260±53 kyr compared to 983 kyr in CK95 and 1159 kyr in GPTS2004

An ~800 kyr interval of strong obliquity-driven cycles in magnetochron C22r marks a critical transition where high-latitude climate processes influenced low-latitude deep ocean chemistry at the onset of Cenozoic cooling
Precession and eccentricity cycles dominate early Eocene climate variability, with the identified 405-kyr long eccentricity cycle providing a stable orbital reference frame for chronostratigraphy

A specific orbital configuration combining low eccentricity with high obliquity amplitudes, coinciding with atmospheric CO₂ dropping below a critical threshold (2-3 times pre-industrial levels), may have triggered the first ephemeral Antarctic ice sheets around 50.1-49.4 Ma

The Green River Formation chronostratigraphy correlation with magnetochron C23n is questionable; radiometric dating of ash layers appears inconsistent with the marine cyclostratigraphy at ODP Site 1258
10.5194/cp-5-309-2009.methodology: X-ray fluorescence (XRF) core scanning of a 120-meter Eocene nannofossil chalk sequence from ODP Site 1258 (Demerara Rise, tropical western Atlantic) was analyzed to construct high-resolution cyclostratigraphy. Spectral analysis using Multi-Taper Method (MTM) and wavelet analysis identified Milankovitch orbital cycles (precession, short/long eccentricity, and obliquity), which were counted and calibrated to magnetostratigraphy to revise magnetochron durations and develop an orbitally-tuned chronostratigraphy.
limitations: Orbital solutions for times older than 42 Ma have limited precision; definite tuning to orbital solutions is not possible, resulting in a floating rather than absolutely-dated timescale
Precession cycle identification is restricted above the C22r/C23n boundary due to lowered sedimentation rates, limiting cyclostratigraphy precision in upper intervals

The Geomagnetic Polarity Time Scale calibration in this interval relies on multiple radiometric age tie-points with large uncertainties, making it difficult to definitively resolve discrepancies between different published timescales

Discrepancies between the ODP Site 1258 cyclostratigraphy and Green River Formation radiometric dating remain unresolved, with no published deep sea record across Chron C22r available for independent verification

The exact position and correlation of ash layers in the Green River Formation to magnetostratigraphy is uncertain due to diachrony between basin center and margin deposits and controversial correlations within the formation
future_work: Resolve pending Green River Formation internal stratigraphy and inconsistencies with pelagic marine cyclostratigraphy and radiometric dating through detailed correlation studies
Develop additional high-resolution cyclostratigraphic records across magnetochron C22r from other deep-sea sites to verify the interpretation of obliquity cycles and sedimentation rates
Refine atmospheric CO₂ reconstruction for the early-middle Eocene transition to better constrain the critical pCO₂ threshold for ice sheet initiation
Investigate the mechanisms by which high-latitude orbital forcing of obliquity was transmitted to low-latitude deep ocean chemistry during the ~800 kyr interval in C22r
Improve astronomical solutions for the early Eocene (pre-42 Ma) to enable absolute dating of cyclostratigraphic frameworks and better test correlations with radiometric records
themes: Early Eocene climate dynamics and Cenozoic cooling
Ice sheet initiation and paleoclimate transitions

10.5194/cp-10-759-2014.title: A seasonality trigger for carbon injection at the Paleocene–Eocene Thermal Maximum
10.5194/cp-10-759-2014.short_summary: This study presents the first high-resolution vegetation and paleoclimate reconstructions for the Paleocene–Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM) using spore and pollen data from the central North Sea. The analysis reveals a significant shift to warmer, wetter conditions, with a marked increase in summer temperatures and precipitation occurring *before* the main negative carbon isotope excursion (CIE). This amplification of seasonal extremes, evidenced by vegetation changes concurrent with early marine ecosystem shifts, provides critical support for the hypothesis that a climatological trigger—such as enhanced seasonal warming destabilizing carbon reservoirs—initiated the massive carbon release that drove the PETM.
key_findings: Amplified seasonal extremes—particularly increases in summer temperatures (>2°C warming) and summer precipitation—preceded the carbon isotope excursion (CIE) at the PETM, suggesting seasonal climate changes triggered carbon injection rather than sustained global warming alone
Vegetation changes occurred prior to the CIE and were concomitant with the occurrence of Apectodinium augustum, representing the earliest sign of PETM-related environmental change in the North Sea

Quantitative paleoclimate reconstructions reveal a shift toward warmer and wetter summers before and during the PETM, with mean annual temperature rising 1-2°C and warm month mean temperature increasing ~2°C prior to the CIE

Within the CIE, dynamic vegetation reorganization occurred: initial increases in epiphytic and climbing ferns followed by extensive wetland development and eventual broadleaf forest (Carya) colonization

Enhanced seasonal precipitation (summer precipitation ~140 mm) corresponds with freshwater indicators (Pediastrum, Bosedinia) indicating increased continental runoff and salinity stratification during the PETM
10.5194/cp-10-759-2014.methodology: High-resolution analysis of spores and pollen assemblages from a well-expanded North Sea core (22/11-N1) combined with nearest living relative (NLR) paleoclimate reconstruction techniques (bioclimatic analysis and mutual climate range method) to estimate mean annual temperature, seasonal temperatures, and precipitation across the PETM interval.
limitations: Large uncertainties in mean annual precipitation (MAP) estimates limit sensitivity to detect significant changes in overall precipitation amounts
Part of the CIE 'body' is missing from the stratigraphic record due to an erosive surface at 2225.2 mbsf
CIE recovery phase is relatively condensed and possibly partially eroded, resulting in incomplete record of the latter interval

Limited to terrestrial vegetation signal transported to marine basin; potential hydrodynamic sorting within turbidity flows, though regional consistency across different depositional settings reduces this concern
Climate estimates may be affected by seasonal bias in oxygen isotope records and summer bias in soil-derived bacterial lipids at high latitudes when compared to other paleoclimate proxies
future_work: Examine whether seasonality-triggered carbon injection mechanism applies to other early Eocene hyperthermal events that appear to be paced by Milankovitch orbital cycles
Investigate coupled atmosphere-ocean GCM models to further test whether enhanced seasonal contrasts through orbital forcing could destabilize submarine methane hydrates globally

Develop higher-resolution records of seasonal climate change in multiple geographic regions to evaluate the universality of the seasonality trigger mechanism across different paleoenvironmental settings
Integrate additional paleoclimate proxy records (e.g., continental paleosols, mammalian fossils) to cross-validate quantitative seasonal climate estimates from palynological data
themes: Carbon cycle perturbations and carbon injection mechanisms
Paleoclimate reconstruction methods (nearest living relative analysis)
Terrestrial vegetation response to rapid warming

10.5194/cp-13-1129-2017.title: Astronomical calibration of the Ypresian timescale: implications for seafloor spreading rates and the chaotic behavior of the solar system?
10.5194/cp-13-1129-2017.short_summary: This study develops the first complete and consistent astronomical timescale for the Ypresian stage (early Eocene, 56–47.8 Ma) by integrating new and existing geochemical, paleomagnetic, and biostratigraphic data from multiple ocean drilling sites. The key findings include resolving a long-standing "50 Ma discrepancy" in the geomagnetic polarity timescale, identifying a 300–400 kyr condensed sedimentary interval, and showing that the geological record best fits the La2010b orbital solution. This correlation suggests a chaotic transition in the solar system's orbital dynamics occurred around 52 Ma. The revised timescale provides precise ages for magnetic reversals, biostratigraphic events, and early Eocene hyperthermal events, offering a crucial framework for studying past climate dynamics.
key_findings: Established the first complete astronomically calibrated timescale (YATS) for the Ypresian stage (56-47.8 Ma) by integrating multi-proxy records (XRF, stable isotopes, magnetostratigraphy, biostratigraphy) from five ODP sites

Resolved the '50 Ma discrepancy' in seafloor spreading rates by demonstrating that Chron C23n was too short at ODP Site 1258; new data reveal a moderate spreading rate increase of 12-19 km/myr at 51.0-52.5 Ma synchronous with global plate-mantle reorganization

Identified geological evidence for chaotic behavior of the solar system by detecting the transition from libration to circulation in Earth-Mars orbital precession at approximately 52 Ma, matching predictions from La2010b/c orbital solutions

Demonstrated that La2010b and La2010c orbital solutions best fit geological data for the Ypresian (46-58 Ma) based on amplitude modulation analysis of eccentricity cycles, while La2004 and La2010d/La2011 solutions diverge beyond 50 Ma

Provided comprehensive calibration of early Eocene hyperthermal events with precise astronomical ages and integrated bio-, chemo-, and magnetostratigraphic framework as reference for paleoclimate studies
10.5194/cp-13-1129-2017.methodology: High-resolution integration of core scanning data (XRF iron intensity), bulk and benthic stable isotope analysis (δ13C, δ18O), paleomagnetic measurements (characteristic remanent magnetization from 400+ samples), and calcareous nannofossil biostratigraphy from five ODP drill sites. Cyclostratigraphic framework established using 405 kyr eccentricity cycles as target, followed by astronomical tuning to orbital solutions and amplitude modulation analysis using statistical methods (Astrochron, ENVELOPE routines).
limitations: Astronomical solutions reliability limited to ~48-50 Ma due to uncertainties in planetary ephemeris; beyond 50 Ma, solutions diverge significantly and 405 kyr cyclostratigraphy becomes primary framework
Magnetostratigraphic interpretation from single sites contains significant errors; multi-site approach required but not all sites provided equally good paleomagnetic signals

Period from 51-52 Ma difficult to tune precisely due to multiple hyperthermal events and shifts in carbon isotope data; nonlinear climate responses complicate direct correlation with orbital solutions

Paired benthic stable isotope records not available for entire Ypresian interval; some hyperthermal events identified only in bulk sediment data, which can be influenced by multiple non-climatic factors
Diachronicity documented for some nannofossil biomarkers (e.g., Blackites inflatus) across geographical regions, suggesting latitude-dependent controls on first appearances
future_work: Higher-resolution paleomagnetic analysis (u-channel sampling) needed to better define magnetic reversals and reduce uncertainty in magnetochronology
Additional high-resolution paired benthic δ13C and δ18O records required to definitively identify hyperthermal events across entire Ypresian and understand climate system response mechanisms
Further exploration of ephemeris differences (INPOP08 vs INPOP10a) to reconcile discrepancies between geological data and theoretical astronomical solutions beyond 50 Ma
Additional work on deep marine and hemipelagic records with good magnetostratigraphy to test consistency and diachroneity of biostratigraphic markers globally
Search for very long eccentricity minima in pre-Ypresian strata (early Paleogene and Mesozoic) to provide landmarks for developing more precise orbital solutions extending further back in time
themes: Paleomagnetic chronostratigraphy and geomagnetic polarity timescale
Early Eocene climate dynamics and hyperthermal events
Orbital mechanics and solar system chaos
Seafloor spreading rates and plate tectonics

10.5194/cp-15-91-2019.title: The 405 kyr and 2.4 Myr eccentricity components in Cenozoic carbon isotope records
10.5194/cp-15-91-2019.short_summary: This study investigates how Earth's orbital eccentricity cycles (405,000 and 2.4 million years) influenced the Cenozoic carbon cycle. Using a carbon cycle model (LOSCAR) forced by orbital variations in organic carbon burial, the authors find the 405-kyr cycle is dominant in oceanic carbon isotopes (δ¹³C), while the longer 2.4-Myr cycle strongly modulates dissolved inorganic carbon and atmospheric CO₂. Analysis of a 22-million-year isotopic record confirms the 405-kyr signal but shows the 2.4-Myr cycle acts mainly as an amplitude modulator of shorter cycles. The work highlights marine organic carbon burial as a key mechanism linking astronomical forcing to carbon cycle dynamics across both ice-free and glaciated climates.
key_findings: The 405 kyr eccentricity cycle is consistently present in Cenozoic δ13C and δ18O records across both ice-free and glaciated climate states, indicating a carbon cycle mechanism independent of ice dynamics

Model simulations show that the 2.4 Myr eccentricity cycle dominates atmospheric CO2 and dissolved inorganic carbon dynamics, while the 405 kyr cycle is more prominent in δ13C, demonstrating differential low-pass filtering of orbital frequencies by different carbon cycle tracers

Simple linear forcing of organic carbon burial in marine sediments with orbital parameters can successfully reproduce the observed 405 kyr cyclicity in paleoclimate records, supporting Corg burial as a plausible mechanism
The 2.4 Myr eccentricity cycle acts primarily as an amplitude modulator of the 405 and 100 kyr eccentricity cycles in the data composite, rather than as a true independent cycle

Silicate weathering (residence time 655 kyr) is the slowest carbon cycle feedback and acts as the primary low-pass filter, explaining why longer eccentricity cycles gain spectral power in total carbon inventory tracers
10.5194/cp-15-91-2019.methodology: The authors used the LOSCAR (Long-term Ocean-atmosphere-Sediment CArbon cycle Reservoir) model to simulate carbon cycle responses to orbital forcing by linearly scaling organic carbon burial rates with various astronomical parameters from Laskar et al. (2004). They compiled a 22 Myr high-resolution composite record of benthic foraminiferal δ13C and δ18O (Eocene-Miocene, 34-12 Ma) and performed multitaper method (MTM) spectral analysis and evolutive harmonics analysis on both model output and paleoclimate data to compare frequency distributions and phase relationships.
limitations: The model uses simplified linear forcing of Corg burial without mechanistic understanding of which specific process controls sediment deposition and clay transport
Large discrepancies exist between modeled δ13C lag times (~190 kyr for 405 kyr cycle) and tuned paleoclimate records (20-60 kyr), indicating incomplete understanding of underlying mechanisms
Available pCO2 proxy records lack sufficient resolution (~200 kyr sampling) and duration to test for 2.4 Myr cyclicity; potential for aliasing
The CCD output is highly variable in the model (10-100 m on 405 kyr timescales) but paleoclimate records show stability, limiting CCD validation
The model uses an artificial climate sensitivity parameterization (5°C per CO2 doubling) that does not capture insolation-driven climate changes independent of pCO2
future_work: Develop long, high-resolution pCO2 proxy records (ideally >5 cycles of 2.4 Myr, spanning 12+ Myr) to test whether atmospheric CO2 is dominated by 2.4 Myr eccentricity cycles as model predictions suggest
Evaluate different magnitudes of spectral power dampening across various carbon cycle variables and paleoclimate proxies using high-resolution records (≥5 times Nyquist frequency for precession)
Investigate mechanistic linkages between eccentricity forcing and sediment deposition/river runoff to replace simplified linear forcing assumptions
Test whether methane hydrate dynamics or other non-linear carbon cycle processes can explain observed 2.4 Myr amplitude modulation patterns in the data
themes: Low-pass filtering and spectral dynamics in geochemical cycles

10.22541/essoar.171259268.89696496/v1.title: Orbital (Hydro)Climate Variability in the Ice-Free early Eocene Arctic
10.22541/essoar.171259268.89696496/v1.short_summary: We explore the imprint of orbital variability on Arctic temperature and hydrology using sediments recovered during the Arctic Coring Expedition in 2004. High resolution records of lipid biomarkers (GDGTs; 2-kyr) and palynological assemblages (5-kyr) in the ~4 m interval below Eocene Thermal Maximum 2 (~54 Ma) show highly cyclic signals related to ~20-kyr precession, ~40-kyr obliquity and ~100-kyr eccentricity. The GDGTs indicate obliquity and precession variability representative of sea surface
key_findings: Orbital cycles (precession, obliquity, eccentricity) imprinted clear signatures on early Eocene Arctic SST and hydrology, with temperature variations of 0.5-1.4°C controlled by these cycles

Early Eocene Arctic precipitation and nutrient supply were driven by regional hydrological processes linked to orbital-forced insolation changes, not meridional water transport as in Pleistocene conditions
Eccentricity-forced polar amplification occurred despite ice-free conditions, indicating non-albedo feedback mechanisms were responsible for enhanced polar temperature variability during the Eocene
10.22541/essoar.171259268.89696496/v1.methodology: High-resolution geochemical and palynological analysis of sediments from the Arctic Coring Expedition, using GDGT lipid biomarkers (2-kyr resolution) and pollen assemblages (5-kyr resolution) to reconstruct orbital-scale variations in sea surface temperature, precipitation, and marine productivity over a ~54 Ma interval.
limitations: Analysis limited to a ~4 m sediment interval below Eocene Thermal Maximum 2
GDGT records show seasonal bias that may preferentially record spring-fall temperatures
Assumption that SST maxima correspond to Arctic insolation maxima may not hold universally
future_work: Investigation of non-ice-albedo feedback mechanisms responsible for polar amplification during greenhouse climates
Extended records across multiple orbital cycles and time intervals to test generalizability of findings
Coupling of Arctic records with deep ocean and tropical paleoclimate data to better constrain global orbital response patterns

10.1073/pnas.2311980121.title: Sensitivity of ocean circulation to warming during the Early Eocene greenhouse
10.1073/pnas.2311980121.venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
10.1073/pnas.2311980121.short_summary: Multiple abrupt warming events (“hyperthermals”) punctuated the Early Eocene and were associated with deep-sea temperature increases of 2 to 4 °C, seafloor carbonate dissolution, and negative carbon isotope (δ 13 C) excursions. Whether hyperthermals were associated with changes in the global ocean overturning circulation is important for understanding their driving mechanisms and feedbacks and for gaining insight into the circulation’s sensitivity to climatic warming. Here, we present high-resol
key_findings: Early Eocene hyperthermals were associated with repeated weakening of global ocean overturning circulation, evidenced by amplified negative δ13C excursions in the deep equatorial Atlantic
Transient warming during the Early Eocene induced weakening of Southern Ocean-sourced overturning circulation and strengthened Atlantic meridional water mass aging gradients
Past patterns of ocean circulation sensitivity to transient warming are consistent with model predictions for declining Atlantic Ocean overturning strength under future climate change scenarios
10.1073/pnas.2311980121.methodology: The study combines high-resolution benthic foraminiferal stable isotope records (δ13C and δ18O) from the Early Eocene Climate Optimum across multiple ocean basins with results from an intermediate complexity Earth system model to demonstrate causal relationships between warming, ocean circulation changes, and carbon cycle signatures.
limitations: Analysis limited to Early Eocene period; results may not directly apply to other greenhouse climates
Reliance on intermediate complexity model which may not capture all processes affecting ocean circulation
Spatial coverage dependent on available paleoceanographic records from sediment cores
future_work: Further investigation of the mechanisms driving hyperthermal events beyond circulation changes
Comparison with other geological warming periods to assess generalizability of circulation sensitivity patterns
Integration of higher-resolution modeling approaches to better capture transient circulation dynamics during warming events
themes: Climate sensitivity and future warming projections

10.1016/j.marpetgeo.2023.106342.title: Organic matter enrichment in Asia's palaeolake controlled by the early and middle Eocene global warming and astronomically driven precessional climate
10.1016/j.marpetgeo.2023.106342.short_summary: Study on Organic matter enrichment in Asia's palaeolake controlled by the early and middle Eocene global warming and astronomically driven precessional climate
key_findings: Global warming during the early and middle Eocene epoch significantly influenced organic matter enrichment in Asian paleolakes
Astronomical forcing, specifically precessional climate cycles, played a controlling role in organic matter accumulation patterns
The interplay between long-term warming trends and short-term orbital climate variations regulated paleolake productivity

10.1029/2006pa001322.title: On the duration of magnetochrons C24r and C25n and the timing of early Eocene global warming events: Implications from the Ocean Drilling Program Leg 208 Walvis Ridge depth transect
10.1029/2006pa001322.short_summary: Five sections drilled in multiple holes over a depth transect of more than 2200 m at the Walvis Ridge (SE Atlantic) during Ocean Drilling Program (ODP) Leg 208 resulted in the first complete early Paleogene deep‐sea record. Here we present high‐resolution stratigraphic records spanning a ∼4.3 million yearlong interval of the late Paleocene to early Eocene. This interval includes the Paleocene‐Eocene thermal maximum (PETM) as well as the Eocene thermal maximum (ETM) 2 event. A detailed chronology
key_findings: High-resolution stratigraphic records from Walvis Ridge provide the first complete early Paleogene deep-sea record, revealing that early Paleogene sedimentary cyclicity is dominated by precession modulated by 100 kyr and 405 kyr eccentricity cycles

The onset of the Paleocene-Eocene thermal maximum (PETM) and Eocene thermal maximum (ETM2) events are related to 100 kyr eccentricity maxima, with different phase relationships to 405 kyr eccentricity cycles

Revised duration estimates for magnetochrons C24r and C25n were established through precession-cycle counting at multiple sites, providing a foundation for developing a cyclostratigraphic framework for the Paleogene
10.1029/2006pa001322.methodology: High-resolution stratigraphic analysis combining nondestructive X-ray fluorescence (XRF) core scanning records and shipboard color data from multiple drill holes at the Walvis Ridge depth transect, with extensive spectral analysis and precession-cycle counting to correlate sedimentary cyclicity patterns with orbital forcing models and establish chronology.
limitations: Absolute age estimates for the PETM, ETM2, and magnetochron boundaries cannot be precisely determined due to large uncertainties in radiometric dating methods
Only two possible tuning options are provided, indicating ambiguity in the final age calibration
future_work: Development of a complete cyclostratigraphic framework based on the stable 405 kyr eccentricity cycle for the entire Paleogene
Refinement of radiometric age calibrations and orbital eccentricity models to enable more precise absolute age determination
themes: Early Paleogene climate events (PETM and ETM2)

10.1002/2016pa003054.title: Orbital forcing of the Paleocene and Eocene carbon cycle
10.1002/2016pa003054.short_summary: This is the final version of the article. Available from the publisher via the DOI in this record.
key_findings: Orbital cycles significantly influence carbon cycle variations during the Paleocene and Eocene epochs
Paleoclimate records show detectable orbital forcing signatures in carbon isotope and carbonate records
Orbital parameters can be used to constrain paleoclimate models and understand past climate sensitivity
10.1002/2016pa003054.methodology: The study likely employs paleoclimate data analysis and modeling to investigate the relationship between orbital forcing and carbon cycle variations during the Paleocene-Eocene interval, using marine sediment records and isotopic proxies.
limitations: Limited to Paleocene-Eocene time periods
Dependent on available paleoclimate proxy records from marine sediments
Complexity of isolating orbital signals from other climate forcing mechanisms
future_work: Higher resolution paleoclimate records could better resolve orbital-scale carbon cycle variations
Integration with additional paleoclimate proxies to strengthen orbital forcing interpretations
Application of improved paleoclimate models to test orbital forcing mechanisms

10.1029/2010pa002092.title: A complete high‐resolution Paleocene benthic stable isotope record for the central Pacific (ODP Site 1209)
10.1029/2010pa002092.short_summary: We have compiled the first stratigraphically continuous high‐resolution benthic foraminiferal stable isotope record for the Paleocene from a single site utilizing cores recovered at Pacific ODP Site 1209. The long‐term trend in the benthic isotope record suggests a close coupling of volcanic CO 2 input and deep‐sea warming. Over the short‐term the record is characterized by slow excursions with a pronounced periodic beat related to the short (100 kyr) and long (405 kyr) eccentricity cycle. The p
key_findings: A high-resolution benthic foraminiferal stable isotope record from the Paleocene reveals a close coupling between volcanic CO2 input and deep-sea warming on long-term timescales
Orbital forcing (eccentricity cycles) acts as a pacemaker for paleoclimatic variability on Milankovitch timescales during the Paleocene, consistent with patterns documented in later epochs
An unusual transient warming event of 2°C at 61.75 Ma coincides with a carbon isotope excursion and marks the onset of long-term δ13C decline, potentially related to Greenland margin volcanism
10.1029/2010pa002092.methodology: High-resolution benthic foraminiferal stable isotope analysis of stratigraphically continuous cores recovered from Pacific ODP Site 1209, with analysis of both long-term trends and short-term periodic variations in relation to orbital cycles.
limitations: Single site study may limit regional applicability
Paleocene record completeness dependent on core recovery quality at one location
future_work: Multi-site comparison to validate orbital forcing patterns identified at ODP Site 1209
Further investigation of the 61.75 Ma warming event and its relationship to Greenland margin volcanism
Extension of high-resolution isotope records to other geological intervals to confirm Milankovitch forcing patterns

10.1029/2018pa003422.title: Orbitally Paced Carbon and Deep‐Sea Temperature Changes at the Peak of the Early Eocene Climatic Optimum
10.1029/2018pa003422.short_summary: Abstract The late Paleocene to early Eocene warming trend was punctuated by a series of orbitally paced transient warming events, associated with the release of isotopically light carbon into the ocean‐atmosphere system. These events occurred throughout the early Eocene, critically persisting during onset, peak, and termination of the early Eocene climatic optimum (EECO) and the onset of the middle Eocene cooling. Here we present a ~5.2‐Myr‐long high‐resolution benthic foraminiferal stable‐isoto
key_findings: High-resolution benthic foraminiferal stable-isotope records from the Early Eocene Climatic Optimum reveal orbitally paced transient warming events associated with isotopically light carbon release into the ocean-atmosphere system

Short-term warming events persisted during the onset, peak, and termination of the EECO, with deep-sea temperature changes during these events consistent with previously documented Eocene thermal maximums

A rapid carbon isotope enrichment of ~1.0‰ at the peak warmth of the EECO (~51.6 Ma) decoupled from climate signals, suggesting a fundamental adjustment in the deep-ocean carbon reservoir due to changes in carbon sources, the biological pump, or ocean circulation
10.1029/2018pa003422.methodology: High-resolution benthic foraminiferal stable-isotope analysis was performed on sediment cores from Ocean Drilling Program Leg 208 Site 1263, examining a ~5.2-million-year record of oxygen and carbon isotopes to characterize temperature changes and carbon cycling during the Early Eocene Climatic Optimum.
limitations: Analysis is limited to a single ODP site, which may not fully represent global oceanographic conditions during the EECO
