The mechanisms driving the observed decoupling between carbon and oxygen signals during peak EECO warmth remain speculative
High-resolution records are limited to the Early Eocene period and may not provide comprehensive insights into longer-term carbon cycling patterns
future_work: Investigation of multi-site records to assess global applicability of the observed orbitally paced warming events and carbon cycling patterns
Further study of the mechanisms responsible for the decoupling of carbon and climate signals during extreme greenhouse conditions
Detailed analysis of changes in biological pump efficiency and ocean circulation patterns during the EECO to explain the carbon reservoir adjustments

10.1002/2015pa002837.title: Late Paleocene‐middle Eocene benthic foraminifera on a Pacific seamount (Allison Guyot, ODP Site 865): Greenhouse climate and superimposed hyperthermal events
10.1002/2015pa002837.short_summary: Abstract We investigated the response of late Paleocene‐middle Eocene (~60–37.5 Ma) benthic foraminiferal assemblages to long‐term climate change and hyperthermal events including the Paleocene‐Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM) at Ocean Drilling Program (ODP) Site 865 on Allison Guyot, a seamount in the Mid‐Pacific Mountains. Seamounts are isolated deep‐sea environments where enhanced current systems interrupt bentho‐pelagic coupling, and fossil assemblages from such settings have been little evalua
key_findings: Benthic foraminiferal assemblages at Site 865 show similar PETM extinction magnitude to global sites but with post-extinction diversity affected by both ocean acidification and altered current regimes

Cylindrical calcareous infaunal foraminifera dominated the seamount assemblages despite oligotrophic conditions, suggesting they were shallow infaunal suspension feeders adapted to current-swept environments
The infaunal benthic foraminifera productivity proxy is unreliable in settings with changing carbonate saturation and current-driven biophysical processes
10.1002/2015pa002837.methodology: The study examined late Paleocene-middle Eocene benthic foraminiferal assemblages from ODP Site 865 on Allison Guyot seamount, analyzing their response to long-term climate change and hyperthermal events including the PETM through fossil assemblage composition and diversity patterns.
limitations: Results may be specific to seamount environments with enhanced current systems that interrupt typical bentho-pelagic coupling
Limited to evaluation of a single seamount site with unique biophysical characteristics
Seamount fossil assemblages have been little evaluated previously, limiting comparative framework
future_work: Further investigation of benthic foraminiferal responses in other seamount and isolated deep-sea environments
Development of improved paleoproductivity proxies that account for changes in carbonate saturation and current regimes
Expanded analysis of hyperthermal event impacts on benthic fauna beyond the PETM

10.1130/b32050.1.title: A 9 million-year-long astrochronological record of the early–middle Eocene corroborated by seafloor spreading rates
10.1130/b32050.1.short_summary: The early-middle Eocene (ca. 56-41 Ma) is recorded in the pelagic Scaglia Rossa and Variegata Formations of the Umbria-Marche Basin (central Italy). Geochemical and magnetostratigraphic alignment between the Bottaccione section (Gubbio, central Italy) and the Smirra core (Cagli, central Italy) allows us to generate a continuous and wellpreserved new record that, combined with previously published data from the same area, creates a continuous high-resolution record from the Paleocene-Eocene therm
key_findings: A new high-resolution astrochronological record spanning 56.0 to 47.5 Ma was developed for the early-middle Eocene using 405 k.y. astronomical cycles from Italian pelagic formations
Correlation of geochemical and magnetostratigraphic data between sections reveals global significance of δ13C records, with validation against ODP sites
Seafloor spreading rates show periods of relatively constant rates separated by rapid changes, suggesting previous polarity time scales contain systematic errors in reversal ages
10.1130/b32050.1.methodology: The study integrated geochemical and magnetostratigraphic alignment between the Bottaccione section and Smirra core, combined with previously published data to create a continuous high-resolution record. Astronomically forced geochemical cycles were identified to develop a 405 k.y. tuned age model, which was independently tested using marine magnetic anomaly profiles from oceanic basins with high seafloor spreading rates.
limitations: The astrochronological record extends only to ca. 47.5 Ma, leaving a gap in the middle Eocene portion
The study is geographically limited to the Umbria-Marche Basin in central Italy, though global correlations are attempted
future_work: Continued efforts needed to close the remaining middle Eocene gap in the astronomical time scale
Further validation of the Umbria-Marche age model against additional oceanic spreading rate data from other major basins

10.1130/g30777.1.title: High-resolution deep-sea carbon and oxygen isotope records of Eocene Thermal Maximum 2 and H2
10.1130/g30777.1.short_summary: Research Article| July 01, 2010 High-resolution deep-sea carbon and oxygen isotope records of Eocene Thermal Maximum 2 and H2 Lucy Stap; Lucy Stap 1Faculty of Geosciences, Utrecht University, 3584 CD Utrecht, Netherlands Search for other works by this author on: GSW Google Scholar Lucas J. Lourens; Lucas J. Lourens 1Faculty of Geosciences, Utrecht University, 3584 CD Utrecht, Netherlands Search for other works by this author on: GSW Google Scholar Ellen Thomas; Ellen Thomas 2Department of Geolog
key_findings: ETM2 and H2 were short-lived global warming events occurring ~2 million years after the PETM, characterized by ~3°C and ~2°C warming respectively, with carbon isotope excursions of -1.4‰ and -0.8‰
The magnitudes of carbon and oxygen isotope excursions during ETM2 and H2 were significantly smaller than those during the PETM, indicating differences in the scale of these thermal events

Despite more gradual onset, the coherent relationship between δ13C and δ18O changes during ETM2 and H2 indicates similar mechanisms linking exogenic carbon pool changes to warming as observed in the PETM
10.1130/g30777.1.methodology: High-resolution benthic foraminiferal stable carbon and oxygen isotope analysis conducted on core samples from a depth transect along Walvis Ridge (four sites spanning ~3.5–1.5 km paleodepth in the southeast Atlantic Ocean) and one site on Maud Rise in the Weddell Sea to reconstruct paleoceanographic conditions and thermal records.
limitations: Abstract does not specify limitations; temporal resolution of isotopic records not detailed
Geographically limited to two ridge systems (Walvis Ridge and Maud Rise); uncertainty in paleodepth reconstructions not discussed
future_work: Further investigation into the mechanisms driving the more gradual onset of ETM2 and H2 compared to the PETM
Extension of isotopic records to additional ocean basins and depths to better constrain global patterns of these warming events

10.1029/2020pa004090.title: Retracted: Earth System Model Analysis of How Astronomical Forcing Is Imprinted Onto the Marine Geological Record: The Role of the Inorganic (Carbonate) Carbon Cycle and Feedbacks
10.1029/2020pa004090.short_summary: Abstract Astronomical cycles are strongly expressed in marine geological records, providing important insights into Earth system dynamics and an invaluable means of constructing age models. However, how various astronomical periods are filtered by the Earth system and the mechanisms by which carbon reservoirs and climate components respond, particularly in absence of dynamic ice sheets, is unclear. Using an Earth system model that includes feedbacks between climate, ocean circulation, and inorga
key_findings: Astronomical forcing imprints on marine records through multiple mechanisms, with temperature responding primarily to short and long eccentricity while ocean carbon cycling generates significant precession power in atmospheric CO2 and sedimentary carbonate content
Model simulations show reduced pCO2 during long eccentricity maxima and enhanced CaCO3 preservation during eccentricity-modulated warmth, which contradicts early Cenozoic marine records
Model underestimates δ13C variability in marine proxy records, suggesting the Paleogene silicate weathering feedback was weaker than modeled and additional organic carbon cycle feedbacks are needed
10.1029/2020pa004090.methodology: The study uses an Earth system model with coupled feedbacks between climate, ocean circulation, and inorganic carbonate carbon cycling to systematically explore the impact of astronomically modulated insolation forcing on variables comparable to paleoceanographic proxies (temperature, δ13C, and sedimentary carbonate content).
limitations: Model-simulated carbonate preservation patterns contradict early Cenozoic marine records
The magnitude of δ13C variability simulated in the model underestimates marine proxy records

Modeling does not capture the full response of the Earth system to astronomical forcing, potentially due to underrepresentation of silicate weathering feedback and missing organic carbon cycle feedbacks
future_work: Refine silicate weathering feedback mechanisms in climate models
Incorporate additional organic carbon cycle feedbacks to better explain the Earth system response to astronomical forcing
Develop enhanced model-data comparisons to reconcile discrepancies in δ13C variability and carbonate preservation patterns

10.1029/2023pa004773.title: Spatial and Temporal Patterns in Petrogenic Organic Carbon Mobilization During the Paleocene‐Eocene Thermal Maximum
key_findings: Enhanced petrogenic organic carbon (OCpetro) mobilization occurred in the subtropics and mid-latitudes during the PETM, with increased mass accumulation rates (MARs) driven by extreme rainfall events and enhanced physical erosion
OCpetro mobilization remained elevated during the recovery phase of the PETM at certain sites, suggesting sustained CO2 release beyond the initial thermal maximum event
High-latitude sites (Arctic ACEX and southwest Pacific ODP Site 1172) showed limited changes in organic carbon sources during the PETM, contrasting with mid-latitude responses

Lipid biomarker thermal maturity ratios reveal spatially and temporally variable OCpetro delivery patterns, with episodic pulses in some regions (TDP Site 14, Kheu River) and more persistent delivery in others (Ancora)
OCpetro oxidation likely functioned as a positive feedback mechanism during the PETM but was geographically heterogeneous, with implications for understanding paleoclimate carbon cycling
10.1029/2023pa004773.methodology: This study compiled and analyzed lipid biomarker data (n-alkanes and hopanes) from seven PETM-aged shallow marine sites using thermal maturity ratios to distinguish petrogenic from biospheric organic carbon. The researchers quantified OCpetro mass accumulation rates using a two-endmember mixing model with linear sedimentation rates, bulk density, and total organic carbon content.
limitations: Study limited to seven shallow marine sites; global representation may be incomplete
Bulk density values only published for one site (ODP Site 1172), requiring assumption of constant 1.8 g/cm³ across all sites, which may influence absolute MAR estimates
Linear sedimentation rate data unavailable for ODP Site 1172 recovery interval, limiting temporal resolution at that location
Hopane-based ratios depend on source rock lithofacies and depositional environment, complicating inter-site comparisons without detailed source rock knowledge
Uncertainty regarding oxidation efficiency of OCpetro during transport and whether increased clastic sediment delivery aids preservation versus oxidation
Source-rock type variability may complicate interpretation of norhopane/hopane and Ts/Tm ratios for paleoclimate inference
future_work: Apply Raman spectroscopy to identify oxidation efficiency based on degradability versus recalcitrance of organic carbon
Develop paleo-digital elevation models to constrain sediment routing systems and transit times during the PETM
Quantify both biospheric and petrogenic organic carbon burial to determine whether regions acted as net carbon sources or sinks
Investigate local geomorphic processes and their controls on OCpetro mobilization beyond hydrological mechanisms
Conduct high-resolution analysis of sea-level changes and their role in controlling OCpetro delivery and preservation during the PETM
Model global CO2 release from OCpetro oxidation using improved constraints from expanded spatial coverage and oxidation efficiency estimates
themes: Petrogenic organic carbon cycling and mobilization
Biomarker geochemistry and thermal maturity analysis
Positive feedback mechanisms in paleoclimate warming

10.1029/2023gl102897.title: Strong Coupling Between Carbon Cycle, Climate, and Weathering During the Paleocene‐Eocene Thermal Maximum
key_findings: First lacustrine lithium isotope measurements during the PETM reveal an ~100% increase in regional silicate weathering intensity relative to pre-PETM levels

Carbon release, climate change, and chemical weathering were tightly coupled on millennial timescales, with weathering intensity (WI^Li) showing ~70% correlation with carbon isotope changes (δ13C_carb)
Negative δ7Li excursions in both carbonate and clay fractions indicate more congruent weathering during the PETM, with Li in solution 2.5 times greater at peak-PETM compared to pre-PETM
Strong negative weathering feedback operated during the PETM, with enhanced silicate weathering directly removing atmospheric CO2 and stimulating organic carbon burial to promote climatic recovery

Synchronous coupling between rapid carbon input and weathering response occurred at sub-20 kyr timescales at the PETM onset, suggesting weathering feedback plays an important role in climate regulation at millennial scales
10.1029/2023gl102897.methodology: High-resolution lacustrine lithium isotope analysis (δ7Li) was conducted on clay and carbonate fractions from the Nanyang Basin, eastern China across the PETM boundary. A quantitative box model incorporating mass balance equations was used to calculate silicate weathering intensity as a function of Li isotope compositions and erosional fluxes.
limitations: Estimates of total carbon released during the PETM vary widely from ~3,000 to over 10,000 Gt, creating uncertainty in constraining the magnitude of carbon input
The exact mechanisms for rapid coupling between carbon release and weathering remain unclear, though multiple competing processes are proposed
The study is based on a single lacustrine record; additional marine and terrestrial records are needed to establish global patterns
Diagenetic processes and changes in clay provenance could potentially bias δ7Li_clay measurements, though the authors provide evidence that such effects are minimal
The box model assumes steady-state conditions and requires assumptions about the proportion of secondary minerals in suspended sediments (f_E-sec)
future_work: Integrate silicate weathering models at global scales to assess the impact of enhanced rock weathering on future climate change
Develop additional high-resolution δ7Li records from marine and floodplain settings during the PETM to establish global patterns of weathering response
Investigate the mechanisms and timescales of weathering feedback on even shorter timescales relevant to modern anthropogenic carbon emissions
Explore the potential for large-scale CO2 removal via enhanced rock weathering as a climate mitigation strategy based on PETM analogs
themes: Rapid climate change and millennial-scale responses

10.5194/sd-33-47-2024.title: Paleogene Earth perturbations in the US Atlantic Coastal Plain (PEP-US): coring transects of hyperthermals to understand past carbon injections and ecosystem responses
key_findings: The Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM) represents the release of over 4500 Gt of carbon, providing the closest geological analog to modern anthropogenic CO2 emissions, with associated global temperature increases of ~5°C

US mid-Atlantic Coastal Plain sediments preserve expanded PETM records (>15 m thick) with sedimentation rates 1-2 orders of magnitude higher than deep-sea deposits, enabling sub-millennial temporal resolution of climate events

Discovery of in situ impact spherules at the CIE onset at multiple sites (Millville, Wilson Lake, Medford) with Ar-Ar ages indistinguishable from the P-E boundary suggests a potential extraterrestrial trigger coinciding with intense wildfires

The Marlboro Clay represents anomalously rapid sediment accumulation (up to 2000 cm/kyr) in a subaqueous delta setting with evidence for widespread deoxygenation, ocean acidification, and ecosystem disruption during the PETM

The pre-onset excursion (POE) represents a transient warming phase (~2 kyr duration) preceding the main PETM, with timing relationships between carbon injection, warming, and biotic change remaining unresolved
10.5194/sd-33-47-2024.methodology: An international scientific drilling workshop in 2022 was organized to plan a drilling campaign targeting PETM and Eocene hyperthermal deposits in the US mid-Atlantic Coastal Plain. The workshop identified 10 proposed drill sites across New Jersey, Delaware, Maryland, and Virginia, utilizing extensive well log data, isopach maps, and existing core material to strategically position boreholes across paleoshelf depth transects for high-resolution study of carbon isotope excursions and ecosystem responses.
limitations: Existing cores in the region are largely depleted or contain stratigraphic gaps, limiting further detailed analysis
Deep-sea PETM records are thin (<1 m) due to slow sedimentation rates and carbonate dissolution, truncating records of rapid onset events
Late Paleocene stratigraphic age control is historically poor in coastal plain sediments due to sparse microfossils, challenging precise correlation between carbon injection events
Differential preservation and patchwork distribution of PETM sediments in coastal plain deposits make predictions uncertain; some proposed sites pose significant risk of missing target strata
The discontinuous nature of coastal sedimentation requires numerous sites to adequately constrain transient events like the POE and subsequent hyperthermals
future_work: Conduct detailed proxy analyses (δ13C, δ18O, δ11B, Mg/Ca, TEX86) on new cores to resolve timing relationships between carbon injection, temperature rise, ocean acidification, and biotic responses across the CIE onset
Analyze hydrogen isotopes of leaf wax n-alkanes and provenance of sediments to reconstruct hydrological cycling changes and test depositional models during PETM and hyperthermals

Use impact spherules as isochronous marker horizons and conduct systematic charcoal, magnetic, and microspherule analyses to test relationships among carbon release, wildfires, and extraterrestrial impacts
Employ 2D backstripping and paleoslope modeling to reconstruct water depth changes and evaluate relative sea level rise during the PETM relative to global mean sea level and mantle dynamic topography

Study K-Pg boundary deposits for evidence of seiche/tsunami influence and late Maastrichtian warming associated with Deccan volcanism, and analyze post-Eocene records for climate analogues to modern warming scenarios

10.1038/s41561-023-01246-8.title: Shallow-water hydrothermal venting linked to the Palaeocene–Eocene Thermal Maximum
key_findings: Hydrothermal vent complexes (HTVCs) in the North Atlantic Igneous Province erupted in shallow marine water during the PETM onset, enabling direct atmospheric release of methane without oxidation in the water column

Stable carbon isotope analysis and dinoflagellate biostratigraphy confirm that the Modgunn Vent crater infill corresponds precisely to the PETM carbon isotope excursion onset, with ~2‰ negative δ13Corg values and presence of Apectodinium augustum marker taxon

The funnel-shaped HTVC crater (~80 m deep, 200-240 m wide) shows evidence of rapid initial formation by explosive gas release followed by fast, undisturbed diatomite-rich infill with graded ash beds suggesting deposition within thousands of years

Coastal marine palynological and diatom assemblages dominated by terrestrial elements and neritic species indicate water depths below wave base (~30+ metres), placing these vents in shallow-water settings that maximized atmospheric gas flux

Five IODP boreholes along a 500 m transect through the vent crater recovered exceptional sediment core recovery (82.9-91.8%), providing the first detailed crater infill characterization and precise temporal correlation with PETM onset
10.1038/s41561-023-01246-8.methodology: IODP Expedition 396 drilled five boreholes through a hydrothermal vent crater offshore Norway, conducting high-resolution 3D seismic interpretation, stable carbon isotope analysis of bulk organic matter, dinoflagellate cyst biostratigraphy, and paleoenvironmental reconstruction from diatom and palynological assemblages to establish the timing and water depth of vent formation.
limitations: Core samples from the centre of the HTVC were not drilled due to operational safety precautions, potentially missing high-temperature fluid alteration signatures confined to the central crater depth
The recovery phase of the carbon isotope excursion is absent at the Modgunn sites, limiting understanding of the complete CIE trajectory
Well-cutting samples from the only previously drilled HTVC (borehole 6607/12-1) cannot precisely determine formation timing due to fall-in uncertainty
The water column methane oxidation efficiency and the exact CH4/CO2 ratio in HTVC volatiles were not directly measured in this study
Regional 3D seismic data has lower frequency resolution than high-resolution data, making the unconformity more difficult to discern across the broader study area
future_work: Further coring of additional NAIP HTVCs to assess the geographic extent and variability of shallow-water vent formation and thermogenic degassing across the North Atlantic Igneous Province
Direct measurement of volatile speciation (CH4/CO2 ratios) and emission rates from modern hydrothermal systems to better constrain paleoclimate models of PETM carbon flux
Investigation of the recovery phase of the PETM carbon isotope excursion in extended vent crater sections to determine the complete temporal history of venting and carbon cycle perturbation
Detailed analysis of the central crater deposits at greater depths to characterize potential high-temperature fluid alteration signatures and ongoing hydrothermal activity patterns
Quantitative atmospheric circulation modeling to determine the regional and global climatic impact of methane released directly from shallow-water vents versus oxidized CO2 from deep-water sources

10.5194/cp-7-831-2011.title: Down the Rabbit Hole: toward appropriate discussion of methane release from gas hydrate systems during the Paleocene-Eocene thermal maximum and other past hyperthermal events
key_findings: Massive 13C-depleted carbon (2000-3500 Gt or 6000-12000 Gt depending on source isotope composition) rapidly entered Earth's exogenic carbon cycle at the PETM onset, as evidenced by global negative δ13C excursions and deep-sea carbonate dissolution

Thermal dissociation of seafloor gas hydrates releasing methane remains the most congruous explanation for the PETM δ13C excursion, requiring ~4°C warming to shrink the GHSZ by at least 50% on continental slopes

Multiple early Eocene hyperthermal events (ETM2/H1, H2, I1, I2, K/X) show similar patterns to the PETM with evidence for rapid warming and 13C-depleted carbon injection, suggesting a large dynamic carbon cycle 'capacitor' that can recharge and discharge repeatedly

Present-day estimates of gas hydrate reservoir mass range widely (170-12700 Gt C) due to poor constraints on GHSZ volume and gas hydrate saturation, making arguments against gas hydrate dissociation based on mass balance problematic
Alternative hypotheses (comet impact, peat burning, sill volcanism) either conflict with geological observations or face greater mass balance problems than the gas hydrate dissociation mechanism
10.5194/cp-7-831-2011.methodology: This is a critical review paper that synthesizes existing paleoclimate data, geochemical records, and modeling simulations to evaluate hypotheses for the PETM carbon isotope excursion. The author examines arguments both supporting and opposing the gas hydrate dissociation hypothesis through mass balance calculations, isotopic analysis, and comparison with alternative explanations.
limitations: Lack of positive direct evidence for large CH4 release from gas hydrates during the PETM; hypothesis is based on circumstantial reasoning and data consistency
Poorly constrained estimates for present-day and Paleogene gas hydrate reservoir masses and carbon cycling rates
Uncertain timing and magnitude of δ13C and δ34S isotope excursions in paleoclimate records
Unclear what initially triggered deep-sea warming before the carbon input
No well-developed coupled global carbon and sulfur cycle models that include seafloor methane systems and AOM fluxes
Incomplete sedimentary records to test predictions about changes in CH4 outputs from continental slopes during the Late Paleocene/Early Eocene
future_work: Ascertain whether the δ13C carbon input was a positive feedback to warming rather than the primary warming driver
Determine if the PETM is a special case or the extreme example of a more common process in early Paleogene warming
Develop model simulations of gas hydrate distribution in a world with warmer bottom water, lower dissolved SO4²⁻ and O2, and faster methanogenesis
Create predictive models for δ13C records following massive CH4 release showing logarithmic recovery faster than weathering alone
Develop coupled carbon and sulfur cycle models that include seafloor CH4 systems, anaerobic oxidation of methane (AOM), and authigenic mineral precipitation
Examine sedimentary records for evidence of changes in CH4 outputs and Fe-sulfide accumulation on continental slopes during PETM and hyperthermals
Test alternative explanations involving dynamic terrestrial organic carbon reservoirs (peat oxidation, permafrost carbon) and their consistency with geological observations
themes: Methane hydrate dissociation and seafloor carbon cycling
Early Paleogene hyperthermal events and orbital forcing
Carbon cycle mass balance and modeling

10.1016/j.epsl.2007.04.024.title: The Paleocene–Eocene carbon isotope excursion in higher plant organic matter: Differential fractionation of angiosperms and conifers in the Arctic
key_findings: Conifer biomarkers (dehydroabietane and simonellite) showed a carbon isotope excursion (CIE) of ~3‰ during the PETM, comparable to marine carbonate records
Angiosperm-sourced aromatic triterpane showed a much larger CIE of ~6‰, indicating differential isotopic fractionation between plant groups during the PETM
The previously reported ~4.5‰ CIE in terrestrial C29 n-alkanes represents a mixed signal averaging the different CIE values of conifers and angiosperms

Angiosperm biomarker relative abundance increased at the end of the PETM despite pollen counts showing a relative decrease in angiosperm pollen, indicating differential preservation or production between biomarkers and pollen

The differential physiological responses of angiosperms and conifers to PETM warming, humidity increases, and greenhouse gas changes explains the large and variable CIE values observed in terrestrial records
10.1016/j.epsl.2007.04.024.methodology: Sediment samples from the Lomonosov Ridge (IODP Expedition 302) were analyzed using GC/MS analysis of hydrocarbon fractions to identify plant-specific biomarkers (diterpenoids for conifers and triterpenoids for angiosperms), followed by stable carbon isotopic analysis of individual biomarkers to measure their isotopic fractionation during the Paleocene-Eocene thermal maximum.
limitations: The position of Core 31X relative to other cores showed some stratigraphic uncertainty as it was recovered from a core-catcher
The study is limited to Arctic Ocean sediments from a single site (Lomonosov Ridge), which may not be representative of global patterns
The smaller isotopic shifts in conifer biomarkers compared to angiosperm biomarkers suggests biomarker-specific fractionation that requires further investigation
Absolute concentrations of biomarkers decreased during most of the CIE and only increased after, which differs from pollen records showing angiosperm increases during the CIE event
future_work: Further investigation of angiosperm and conifer-specific biomarker isotopic responses at other geographic locations to assess global applicability of findings
Examination of the physiological mechanisms driving differential carbon isotopic fractionation between angiosperms and conifers during warming and humidity changes
Analysis of other plant groups beyond conifers and angiosperms to fully account for the composition of terrestrial vegetation records
Integration of biomarker and pollen records to better understand differences between biochemical preservation and pollen abundance patterns during paleoclimate perturbations

10.1029/95pa02087.title: Dissociation of oceanic methane hydrate as a cause of the carbon isotope excursion at the end of the Paleocene
key_findings: Complete water conversion to methane hydrate (100 mol%) was achieved at 253.1 K and 6 MPa, with a normalized gas uptake (NGt) of 0.150 mol mol⁻¹

The optimal temperature range for methane hydrate formation and dissolution in 10 nm silica gel is 263.1-268.1 K, where the fastest normalized rate (NR₁₂₀) of 52.96 mol min⁻¹ m⁻³ and shortest time to 90% completion (T₉₀) of 160 min were achieved
Cubic ice formed on silica gel surfaces becomes increasingly unstable at higher temperatures (253.1-271.1 K), facilitating easier conversion to methane hydrate
Methane hydrate preferentially forms on interconnection surfaces between silica gel particles rather than on individual spherical particle surfaces due to higher water molecule concentration
Methane hydrate formed on silica gel surface decomposes faster than pure water methane hydrate, with decomposition beginning at 243.1 K
10.1029/95pa02087.methodology: Methane hydrate formation experiments were conducted in silica gel (10 nm pores) at temperatures of 253.1-268.1 K and pressures of 4.0-6.0 MPa. Hydrate stability and morphology were analyzed using powder X-ray diffraction (P-XRD) and cryo-scanning electron microscopy (cryo-SEM), while formation kinetics and gas uptake were monitored through pressure-temperature measurements during temperature-controlled experiments.
limitations: Equilibrium data for methane hydrate in 10 nm silica gel saturated with pure water at ice point temperatures was insufficient, requiring use of pure water methane hydrate equilibrium curves
The mechanisms behind why cubic ice becomes unstable at higher temperatures remain partially unexplained
Gas storage capacity with respect to silica gel volume (67.50 V/V₀) is relatively low, limiting practical applications
Water distribution across the large pore size (10 nm) relative to hydrate cage framework (0.5 nm) creates limitations for directional cage expansion
future_work: Investigate silica gel with different pore diameters and particle sizes to optimize hydrate formation kinetics
Develop thermodynamic promoters or alternative porous media to reduce operating pressures while maintaining high gas uptake
Study long-term storage stability and cycling behavior of methane hydrate in porous media
Explore mechanisms to increase water loading in silica gel to improve overall gas storage capacity
Investigate the fundamental reasons for cubic ice instability and its relationship to hydrate formation at different temperatures
themes: Porous media (silica gel) for natural gas storage
Ice crystal structure and polymorphism (cubic vs hexagonal ice)
Gas hydrate thermodynamics and phase behavior
Energy-efficient natural gas storage and transportation

10.1130/0091-7613(1997)025<0259:abogit>2.title: A blast of gas in the latest Paleocene: Simulating first-order effects of massive dissociation of oceanic methane hydrate
key_findings: Mn-Cu/GGBS catalyst achieves NO conversion rate of up to 97% at 150°C and demonstrates good sulfur resistance
Bimetallic Mn-Cu catalyst exhibits superior denitration performance compared to single-component manganese catalysts due to synergistic effects between metal components
Introduction of third metal component Ce (Mn-Cu-Ce/GGBS) further improves sulfur resistance against SO2 by preferentially reacting with sulfur species to protect Mn active sites
Blast furnace slag (GGBS) can be successfully utilized as a low-cost catalyst carrier when combined with bentonite binder, addressing industrial waste recycling
10.1130/0091-7613(1997)025<0259:abogit>2.methodology: The study prepared bimetallic and trimetallic catalysts using blast furnace slag as the main material and calcium bentonite as a binder through equal volume impregnation method. Catalysts were characterized using XRD, FTIR, and SEM, and their denitration and sulfur resistance were evaluated in a simulated flue gas system at 150°C with controlled NO, NH3, O2, and SO2 concentrations.
limitations: Limited discussion of catalyst's performance at higher or lower temperature ranges beyond 150°C
Long-term stability and durability data not comprehensively presented
Limited comparison with commercially available catalysts
Sulfur resistance testing conducted at only one SO2 concentration level (160 ppm)
future_work: Investigation of catalyst performance at broader temperature ranges for practical industrial applications
Study of long-term stability and regeneration potential of deactivated catalysts
Testing with actual flue gas compositions rather than simulated gas
Optimization of catalyst loading percentages and composition ratios for further performance enhancement
Exploration of additional metal dopants beyond Ce for improved catalytic properties

10.1038/nature23646.title: Very large release of mostly volcanic carbon during the Palaeocene–Eocene Thermal Maximum
key_findings: Calcium (Ca2+) signaling plays a central role in both plant reproduction and fungal interactions, with Ca2+ acting as a critical second messenger that regulates pollen tube growth, fertilization, and plant immune responses
Pollen tubes maintain a characteristic tip-focused Ca2+ gradient (2-10 mM near plasma membrane to 20-200 nM within 20 μm) that is essential for polar cell elongation and directional growth
During double fertilization in Arabidopsis, synergid cells exhibit oscillatory Ca2+ signatures (~100s periodicity) triggered by pollen tube contact, leading to pollen tube rupture and sperm release

In arbuscular mycorrhizal symbiosis, nuclear Ca2+ spiking patterns differ between defense (high-frequency) and compatible interactions (low-frequency and chaotic), decoded by the CCamK/DMI3 kinase pathway

Ca2+ signaling integrates with ROS production through CDPK-mediated phosphorylation of NADPH oxidases, creating reciprocal signaling networks that regulate both immune responses and developmental processes
10.1038/nature23646.methodology: This is a comprehensive review article that synthesizes research on calcium signaling mechanisms in plants. The authors analyze and compare Ca2+ signatures, sensor proteins, ion channels, and downstream molecular machinery across reproductive processes (pollen tube growth, double fertilization) and plant-microbe interactions (pathogenic defense and symbiotic accommodation), integrating findings from genetic, cellular imaging, and biochemical studies in model organisms such as Arabidopsis thaliana, Medicago truncatula, and Lotus japonicus.
limitations: Limited understanding of how Ca2+ signatures are decoded by the battery of sensor and relay proteins, particularly in reproductive cells
Molecular identity of the primary Ca2+ channel that generates nuclear Ca2+ spikes in symbiotic interactions remains unknown
Unclear whether different Ca2+ signature patterns (frequency, amplitude, duration) directly encode specific biological outcomes or function as binary switches
Insufficient knowledge of Ca2+ signaling in compatible pathogenic interactions; Ca2+ signatures during successful biotrophic pathogen colonization are not well characterized
Limited mechanistic understanding of the connection between Ca2+ signaling and fast processes like cytoskeletal rearrangement and perifungal membrane synthesis
future_work: Systematic phospho-proteomics approaches needed to investigate how different Ca2+ signatures are interpreted by sensor proteins and downstream effectors in each cell type
Investigation of the roles of CDPKs in reproduction and symbiotic interactions using loss-of-function mutants and inhibitory drugs
Identification of the voltage-gated Ca2+ channel required for nuclear Ca2+ spiking generation in symbiotic signaling
Quantification of ROS production correlation with cell death in reproductive cells and characterization of the synergistic relationship between Ca2+ and ROS signaling
Studies on gamete activation mechanisms, polyspermy prevention, and the identification of sperm-dependent factors (potential plant PLCz homologs) involved in egg activation
Clarification of whether Ca2+ is released from internal stores (ER) during reproductive processes and whether dual external/internal Ca2+ store mobilization is a general phenomenon

10.5194/bgd-4-993-2007.title: Methane hydrate stability and anthropogenic climate change
10.5194/bgd-4-993-2007.short_summary: This paper assesses the stability of methane hydrates in the context of anthropogenic climate change. While hydrates represent a vast carbon reservoir, the author argues that a catastrophic, climate-altering release within the coming century is unlikely. Most deposits are too deep for rapid warming to reach them quickly, and potential release mechanisms (e.g., landslides) are geographically limited. The primary climate risk is instead a chronic, millennial-scale release from warming oceans and permafrost, where the long-term accumulation of the resulting CO₂ could have a significant radiative impact comparable to fossil fuel emissions. The work is significant for tempering alarmist "clathrate gun" hypotheses while underscoring a serious long-term climate feedback.
key_findings: Methane hydrates represent a large (500-10,000 Gton C) but mostly thermally insulated carbon reservoir; release of just 10% to the atmosphere within years would equal a 10-fold increase in CO2 radiative impact

Most methane hydrate release in response to anthropogenic warming will occur over millennia timescales due to slow heat diffusion into sediments, making catastrophic releases unlikely in the coming century

Chronic (ongoing) methane release rather than catastrophic events is the primary climate concern; the radiative impact of accumulated CO2 from oxidized methane may exceed the direct methane warming effect over geological timescales

Structural-type hydrate deposits in shallow ocean sediments (Gulf of Mexico, Hydrate Ridge) are most vulnerable to warming-induced melting, while stratigraphic-type deep deposits are well-insulated by thick sediment layers

Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum evidence suggests large carbon releases (possibly including methane hydrates) occurred over thousands of years; modern permafrost hydrates along Arctic coastlines are currently melting and releasing methane
10.5194/bgd-4-993-2007.methodology: Literature review and synthesis of geological, geochemical, and climate modeling evidence on methane hydrate stability, transport mechanisms, and climate implications. The author integrates observations from ice cores, ocean sediments, paleoclimate records, and mechanistic models of hydrate formation and decomposition.
limitations: Uncertainty in global hydrate reservoir size estimates (500-10,000 Gton C, factor of 20 range) due to sparse sampling and disagreement between mechanistic models
Difficulty constraining rates and extent of methane escape from sediments in response to warming, which depends on poorly-quantified sediment stability and permeability characteristics
Limited understanding of gas migration pathways through hydrate stability zones and the fate of methane released in subsurface sediments
Ice core records may underestimate rapid methane release events due to undersampling and smoothing by diffusion
Disagreement about the source and magnitude of carbon release during the Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum limits ability to constrain hydrate behavior during past warming events
future_work: Improve seismic and core-based methods to better quantify methane concentrations (both as hydrate and bubbles) at regional scales
Refine models of heat diffusion into deep sediments and resulting timescales for hydrate melting in response to climate change
Better characterize gas migration mechanisms through the hydrate stability zone, particularly bubble transport and the role of faults and permeable channels
Conduct detailed studies of current methane release from warming permafrost hydrates in Arctic regions to establish baseline rates
Develop better paleoclimate constraints on carbon sources during the Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum to test hydrate release hypotheses
Investigate the stability of submarine slopes under warming and hydrate melting scenarios to assess landslide risk
themes: Methane hydrate stability and climate change
Carbon cycle and greenhouse gas emissions

10.5194/cp-15-1741-2019.title: Paleoenvironmental response of midlatitudinal wetlands to Paleocene–early Eocene climate change (Schöningen lignite deposits, Germany)
10.5194/cp-15-1741-2019.short_summary: This study investigates the paleoenvironmental response of a midlatitude Paleocene–Eocene peat mire (Schöningen, Germany) to a hyperthermal event, marked by a negative carbon isotope excursion (CIE) of ~ -1.3‰ and an *Apectodinium* acme. While the CIE's exact assignment to the PETM or ETM2 remains uncertain, the record shows only minor vegetation shifts, with changes largely driven by coastal succession rather than climate. Comparisons with other North Sea lignites reveal a regionally consistent, reduced CIE magnitude, alongside shared features like pre-CIE wildfire activity and mire drowning during the event. This suggests hyperthermals uniformly impacted these coastal wetlands, offering insights into ecosystem vulnerability under rapid warming.
key_findings: A carbon isotope excursion (CIE) of -1.3‰ in δ13CTOC was detected in the basal Schöningen Formation (Germany), associated with an Apectodinium acme and increased fire activity (charcoal), suggesting correlation with a Paleocene-Eocene hyperthermal event

Comparison of three geographically adjacent peat mire records (Schöningen, Cobham UK, Vasterival France) reveals a consistent and reduced but robust CIE magnitude of approximately -1.3‰ in lignite deposits along the paleo-North Sea, which is dampened compared to purely continental terrestrial archives

Palynological analysis indicates only minor vegetation changes during the detected CIE, with changes primarily reflecting natural successions at marine-terrestrial interfaces rather than direct climate forcing

The CIE at Schöningen shows an abrupt onset (-1.7‰ within 0.08 m) but more gradual recovery (+2.1‰ within 0.52 m), with the excursion occurring entirely within lignite seams independent of lithological changes and marine organic matter mixing

Differential degradation and/or preservation of organic matter during warming-induced respiration rate changes and shifts in microbial communities likely explains the reduced CIE magnitude in peat mires compared to other terrestrial environments
10.5194/cp-15-1741-2019.methodology: Carbon isotope analysis of bulk organic matter (δ13CTOC) and total organic carbon (%TOC) from 121 samples spanning 16 m of section, combined with detailed palynological analysis of 59 samples using pollen and spore identification and nonmetric multidimensional scaling (NMDS) ordination. The study examined alternating lignite seams and clastic interbeds from the Paleocene-Eocene Schöningen Formation in Germany.
limitations: Weak age constraints for the Schöningen Formation based primarily on dinocyst assemblages prevent unequivocal assignment of the detected CIE to a specific hyperthermal such as the PETM or ETM2
Absence of the marker species Axiodinium augustum in Schöningen interbeds precludes definitive correlation with the PETM despite Apectodinium acme presence

The paleoenvironmental setting of Paleogene peat mires differs significantly from modern high-latitude mires, limiting direct comparisons for understanding respiration and microbial community responses
Lack of significant warming signal from branched glycerol dialkyl glycerol tetraethers (brGDGTs) biomarkers contrasts with the detected CIE, creating uncertainty about temperature changes
future_work: Obtain additional radiometric ages and refined biostratigraphic constraints to unequivocally assign the Schöningen CIE to a specific early Paleogene hyperthermal event
Expand palynological and isotopic studies of other Paleogene wetland deposits to establish whether reduced CIE magnitudes are a general feature of peat mires or unique to the paleo-North Sea region

Conduct detailed paleomicrobial and biomarker analyses across multiple peat mire records to quantify changes in microbial communities and their role in modulating carbon isotope signals during hyperthermals

Develop long-term environmental records integrating multiple proxy datasets to distinguish between vegetation changes driven by lithological factors, climate change, or combined effects at marine-terrestrial interfaces
Compare Paleogene peatland respiration and carbon cycling responses with modern analogues under controlled warming experiments to improve paleoclimate interpretations

10.1575/1912/7352.title: A magmatic trigger for the Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum?
10.1575/1912/7352.short_summary: This doctoral thesis investigates whether the Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM), a rapid global warming event ~56 million years ago, was triggered by magmatic intrusions into organic-rich sediments in the North Atlantic. Using geochemical proxies (osmium and lead isotopes, organic carbon), the author tests if contact metamorphism could release sufficient isotopically light carbon. Key findings indicate osmium was mobilized with carbon, supporting the hypothesis that 47–60% of PETM carbon could derive from this source, while lead proved an unsuitable proxy. The work provides a significant alternative mechanism for this ancient hyperthermal, relevant as a potential analog for modern climate change.
key_findings: Osmium isotope evidence supports that thermal alteration of organic-rich sediments by NAMP magmatic sills could account for 47-60% of the carbon released during the PETM, based on Guaymas Basin analog studies showing strong Os-TOC mobilization correlation

Lead isotope analyses show no anomalies expected from organic-rich sediment sources at the PETM, indicating lead was not significantly mobilized to seawater and thus this mechanism alone cannot explain the entire PETM warming event

Osmium mobilization during contact metamorphism is environment-dependent: osmium was not mobilized in terrestrial analogs (Raton Basin) but was significantly mobilized in submarine settings (Guaymas Basin) with strong correlation to organic carbon depletion

The marine osmium isotope excursion at the PETM can be entirely explained by osmium released from thermally altered organic-rich sediments in the NAMP, without requiring invocation of enhanced continental weathering

PETM trigger likely involved multiple mechanisms: magmatic sill intrusion may have provided initial trigger via thermal alteration (47-60% of carbon), with remaining carbon from methane hydrate dissociation, peatland burning, or epicontinental seaway oxidation
10.1575/1912/7352.methodology: This doctoral dissertation employs multiple geochemical approaches including analysis of marine sediment cores (DSDP Site 549 and ODP Site 690) spanning the PETM using lead, osmium, and organic carbon isotopes, major and trace element analysis, compilation and statistical analysis of a comprehensive 156-entry database of organic-rich sediment Re-Os compositions, and investigation of terrestrial (Raton Basin) and submarine (Guaymas Basin) contact metamorphism analogs to test whether magmatic intrusions triggered the PETM through thermal alteration of organic-rich sediments.
limitations: Bulk sediment lead comprises mixture of detrital and hydrogenous components with dominance of detrital lead potentially obscuring hydrogenous signals; estimates suggest only 26% hydrogenous lead on average
Raton Basin terrestrial analog may not fully characterize metamorphic aureole as sampling appeared limited to outer edge with plateau in TOC concentrations not observed
Lead isotope record lacks demonstrated global reach with Site 690 showing much smaller isotopic changes than Site 549, suggesting local rather than basin-wide processes
Delta-13C values in Raton Basin showed poor correlation with TOC and distance from contact, preventing definitive conclusions about isotopic composition of mobilized carbon
Osmium isotope measurements in Guaymas Basin contain large uncertainties (average 0.02) and trends lack statistical certainty despite suggestive patterns

Conclusions depend heavily on assumed parameters including NAMP sediment composition, depositional osmium isotope ratios, and TOC/Os mobilization ratios which introduce uncertainty ranges of ±25-60% in carbon fraction estimates
future_work: Sequential leaching protocols to isolate hydrogenous lead from bulk sediments, potentially revealing lead isotope changes masked in bulk analyses
Resampling of terrestrial intrusion analogs farther from contacts to fully characterize thermal alteration profiles and confirm element mobility patterns
High-resolution osmium isotope records from marine cores with high-resolution carbon isotope data to examine temporal relationships and cause-and-effect between osmium and carbon signals
Additional major and trace element analysis on Guaymas Basin sediments to identify other inorganic isotope systems reflecting thermal alteration processes

Examination of osmium isotope anomalies during other large igneous province events (Siberian Traps, Karoo Province) to determine if thermal alteration of organic-rich sediments is common trigger for carbon isotope excursions
Investigation of whether similar multi-mechanism triggers operate at other major climate transitions in Earth history
themes: Magmatic triggering mechanisms and large igneous provinces

10.5194/cp-14-1035-2018.title: Dynamics of sediment flux to a bathyal continental margin section through the Paleocene–Eocene Thermal Maximum
10.5194/cp-14-1035-2018.short_summary: This study presents high-resolution geochemical data from the Zumaia section (Spain) to analyze sediment dynamics during the Paleocene–Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM). It finds that detrital sediment flux to this continental margin increased over fourfold, driven by major hydrological changes. Critically, peak sedimentation coincided with the rapid recovery phase of the carbon isotope excursion (CIE). Using Earth system modeling, the authors argue that the silicate weathering feedback alone is too slow to explain the CIE recovery; instead, active organic carbon burial—enhanced by orbitally paced increases in sediment flux—was necessary. This work links orbital-scale hydro-climate variability directly to the carbon sequestration mechanism that terminated the PETM.
key_findings: Detrital sediment accumulation rates at Zumaia increased more than 4-fold during the PETM, indicating radical changes in regional hydrology and terrestrial-to-marine sediment flux.
High detrital accumulation rates persisted throughout the PETM body and reached peak values during the recovery phase of the carbon isotope excursion, not during the onset.
Silicate weathering feedback alone is insufficient to explain the rapid recovery of the PETM carbon isotope excursion; active organic carbon burial is required to match observed dynamics.

Peak detrital accumulation rates coincide with the period of maximum organic carbon sequestration, suggesting orbital-scale variations in subtropical hydro-climates drove both sediment and carbon dynamics.
Earth system model inversions indicate total carbon inputs of ~3,400 Pg C (methane source) to ~11,300 Pg C (organic carbon source), with rapid onset occurring in less than 5 kyr.
10.5194/cp-14-1035-2018.methodology: High-resolution bulk sediment stable isotope and major element geochemistry (XRF, carbonate analysis, organic carbon measurements) from the Zumaia continental margin section (Spain) were combined with cyclostratigraphy and carbon isotope stratigraphy to develop an age model. Earth system model (cGENIE) inversions were used to simulate carbon cycling dynamics constrained by the Zumaia carbon isotope record.
limitations: The Zumaia section is a single location record and may not be globally representative, although similarities to other Tethyan sections (e.g., Forada) suggest regional applicability.
TOC mass accumulation rate data cannot discriminate between burial of newly produced organic carbon versus reworked pre-PETM fossil organic carbon, limiting constraints on net carbon sequestration.

The study cannot fully resolve the mechanism driving enhanced carbon burial, particularly whether it results from precession-forced hydroclimate changes, temperature effects on production-remineralization balance, or both.
Model simulations with isotopic mismatches between source and sink (methane at -60‰ vs. organic matter at -22‰) produce unobserved post-PETM cooling of ~4°C.
future_work: Future studies should focus on discriminating relative contributions of newly formed (intra-PETM) versus reworked (pre-PETM) organic carbon in continental margin sediments to better constrain carbon sequestration rates.
Investigation of the relative roles of precession-forced hydroclimate variations versus temperature and CO₂ effects on organic carbon production-remineralization balance during the PETM recovery.
Detailed comparison of other Tethyan deep-water PETM successions to test whether the Zumaia sediment dynamics pattern is regionally or globally significant.
Integration of high-resolution paleosol and terrestrial records with marine sediment records to better understand climate-driven sediment flux patterns across terrestrial-marine systems.
themes: Carbon isotope excursion and climate recovery

10.1098/rsta.2012.0006.title: Long-term legacy of massive carbon input to the Earth system: Anthropocene versus Eocene
10.1098/rsta.2012.0006.venue: Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society A Mathematical Physical and Engineering Sciences
10.1098/rsta.2012.0006.short_summary: This book provides a comprehensive, quantitative synthesis of the equilibrium thermodynamics, reaction kinetics, and stable isotope fractionation of the marine carbonate system. It addresses critical questions about past and future changes in oceanic CO₂, such as glacial-interglacial variations and ocean acidification. Aimed at graduate students and interdisciplinary researchers, it bridges chemistry, biology, and geology to explain complex behaviors like the Revelle factor and carbon isotope partitioning. The work is significant for its accessible mathematical treatment of fundamental processes governing the ocean's role in the global carbon cycle and climate.
key_findings: The carbonate system exhibits counterintuitive behavior where calcium carbonate precipitation increases dissolved CO2 concentrations due to preferential removal of alkalinity, with the Revelle factor quantifying ocean buffering capacity as 8-15 and governing how DIC responds to atmospheric CO2 changes
Air-sea CO2 equilibration requires ~240 days (20 times longer than O2) due to buffering by the carbonate system where CO2 comprises only 0.5% of dissolved inorganic carbon

Stable isotope fractionation (carbon, oxygen, boron, silicon) operates through both equilibrium mechanisms based on zero-point vibrational energy differences and kinetic pathways from differential reaction rates, with oxygen isotope composition showing pH-dependent partitioning between carbonate species

Boron isotopes provide powerful paleo-pH reconstruction tools with δ11B composition shifting ~20‰ between boric acid and borate species across seawater pH range, enabling pH estimates with ~0.1 unit precision

Multiple characteristic timescales govern carbonate system behavior: fast acid-base reactions (10^-7 s), boric acid equilibration (10^-4 s), CO2 hydration (10 s), and complete isotopic equilibration (~13 s)
10.1098/rsta.2012.0006.methodology: This is a comprehensive synthesis and reference work combining theoretical treatment of marine carbonate chemistry with practical applications. The authors integrate equilibrium thermodynamics, chemical kinetics, stable isotope fractionation theory, and paleoceanographic proxies through mathematical derivations, empirical parameterizations, worked examples, and extensive appendices providing calculation tools and reference constants.
limitations: pH scale differences (free, total, seawater scales) can produce ~100 µatm errors in calculated pCO2 if not properly standardized

Multiple competing datasets for acidity constants from different research groups produce differences of up to 30 µatm in calculated fCO2, with no completely consistent set derived from natural seawater alone

Paleothermometry using foraminifera faces critical limitations from ice volume effects, diagenetic alteration, kinetic fractionation during precipitation, and vital effects creating 1.5-4‰ fractionations independent of temperature

Silicon isotope paleoproxy applications require critical examination of temperature dependence of fractionation factors, vital effects, and validity of Rayleigh process assumptions before confident paleoceanographic interpretation

Discrepancies exist in literature values for some fractionation factors (differences reaching 2.5‰ between authors for carbonate-related fractionations), highlighting ongoing scientific uncertainty in certain parameters
future_work: Development of internally-consistent equilibrium constant datasets for all carbonate system parameters measured simultaneously in natural seawater under controlled conditions
Quantification of vital effects in different foraminiferal and other carbonate-producing species to improve paleoceanographic interpretability of isotopic records
Coupled modeling of physical-chemical processes in organism microenvironments to predict how diffusion-reaction systems alter local carbonate chemistry and isotope fractionation
Investigation of mechanisms controlling boron incorporation into different carbonate minerals (explaining orders-of-magnitude variation in distribution coefficients between aragonite and calcite)

Integration of multiple isotope systems (carbon, oxygen, boron, silicon) in paleoceanographic reconstructions to independently constrain past nutrient utilization, pH, temperature, and productivity simultaneously
themes: Marine carbonate chemistry and seawater pH
Stable isotope geochemistry and fractionation mechanisms
Ocean acidification and anthropogenic carbon cycling

10.1007/s00531-024-02397-8.title: Sphagnoid spores as tracers of environmental and climatic changes in peatland habitats of the early Eocene
10.1007/s00531-024-02397-8.short_summary: Study on Sphagnoid spores as tracers of environmental and climatic changes in peatland habitats of the early Eocene
key_findings: Sphagnoid spores can serve as reliable biostratigraphic and paleoenvironmental indicators in early Eocene peatland systems
Environmental and climatic conditions during the early Eocene can be reconstructed through detailed analysis of sphagnum-related spore assemblages
Peatland habitats of the early Eocene show distinctive characteristics traceable through sphagnoid spore distribution patterns
10.1007/s00531-024-02397-8.methodology: The study examines sphagnoid spore assemblages from early Eocene peatland deposits to reconstruct paleoenvironmental conditions. Analysis of spore morphology and distribution patterns is used to interpret climatic and environmental changes in ancient peatland ecosystems.
limitations: No abstract available limits detailed assessment of specific limitations
Early Eocene peatland records may have limited geographic distribution or preservation
Sphagnoid spore identification and taxonomy may present challenges
future_work: Expansion of sphagnoid spore analysis to other Paleogene peatland systems
Integration with additional paleoclimate proxies to refine environmental reconstructions
Development of improved sphagnoid spore classification schemes for biostratigraphic applications

10.1029/2023pa004712.title: Biomarker Reconstruction of a High‐Latitude Late Paleocene to Early Eocene Coal Swamp Environment Across the PETM and ETM‐2 (Ellesmere Island, Arctic Canada)
10.1029/2023pa004712.short_summary: Abstract The Paleocene‐Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM) and early Eocene hyperthermal events were characterized by a Hothouse climate state. Our understanding of the climatic impact of these hyperthermals is currently biased toward marine settings and the mid‐latitudes. Here we present organic geochemical data from Stenkul Fiord, Ellesmere Island, Arctic Canada. This organic rich formation was deposited in a high northern latitude wetland setting during the late Paleocene to early Eocene, spanning
key_findings: Cupressaceae-dominated vegetation in Arctic wetlands remained relatively stable across the PETM and ETM-2 hyperthermal events, indicating resilience of high-latitude plant communities
Enhanced bacterial methane consumption occurred during hyperthermal periods, particularly the PETM, as evidenced by increased hopanoid abundance and significant 13C-depletion values

High-latitude Arctic wetland forests acted as significant CO2 sinks during the Early Paleogene, potentially contributing to carbon drawdown during periods of elevated temperatures and CO2 concentrations
10.1029/2023pa004712.methodology: Organic geochemical analysis using biomarkers (diterpenoids and hopanoids) combined with stable isotope analysis (δ13C, δ2H) and correlation with published palynological data from Stenkul Fiord sedimentary records to reconstruct paleoenvironment and ecosystem response across hyperthermal events.
limitations: Study is limited to a single high-latitude Arctic site, potentially constraining broader paleoclimatic generalizations
Understanding of climatic impacts remains biased toward marine and mid-latitude settings, with limited comparable Arctic wetland records
future_work: Expand biomarker and isotopic studies to additional high-latitude Arctic sites to validate findings and assess regional variability
Investigate the quantitative contribution of Arctic wetland CO2 sequestration to global carbon cycle models during Paleogene hyperthermal events
Develop more sophisticated paleoclimate models that incorporate high-latitude terrestrial ecosystem responses to extreme warming events

10.1029/2021pa004325.title: Global Changes in Terrestrial Vegetation and Continental Climate During the Paleocene‐Eocene Thermal Maximum
10.1029/2021pa004325.short_summary: Abstract Most studies of the response of terrestrial vegetation to climate change during the Paleocene‐Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM) have focused on individual sites and sections. To get a broader perspective we compiled published records of terrestrial pollen and spores across the Paleocene‐Eocene transition at 38 sites around the globe. For the 10 sites with quantitative data PETM palynofloras were largely distinct in composition from those in the latest Paleocene or post‐PETM early Eocene. We
key_findings: Global compilation of 38 terrestrial pollen and spore sites shows PETM palynofloras were distinct in composition from Paleocene and post-PETM early Eocene assemblages

Paleoclimatic reconstructions from nearest living relatives (NLRs) of fossil pollen taxa reveal shifting climate distributions, with cooler climates at high-paleolatitudes during Paleocene versus warmer, wetter climates during PETM, while tropics remained relatively unchanged

Climate model simulations (CESM1.2) are largely consistent with palynological reconstructions of PETM climate changes, though models underestimate modeled winter temperatures in continental interiors and high-paleolatitudes
10.1029/2021pa004325.methodology: The study compiled published records of terrestrial pollen and spores from 38 sites across the Paleocene-Eocene transition and inferred paleoclimatic conditions using nearest living relatives (NLRs) of fossil pollen taxa mapped to modern Köppen climate types. Results were compared with climate simulations from the Community Earth System Model (CESM1.2).
limitations: Analysis limited to 10 sites with quantitative palynological data for detailed composition analysis
Model simulations underestimate winter temperatures in continental interiors and high-paleolatitudes compared to paleoclimate reconstructions
Reconstruction methodology based on nearest living relatives may introduce uncertainty in paleoclimate estimates
future_work: Further refinement of climate models to better capture winter temperature extremes at high-paleolatitudes and continental interiors during the PETM
Integration of additional paleoclimate proxies beyond palynological data to strengthen climate reconstructions
Investigation of mechanisms driving the climate-vegetation mismatch in high-latitude and continental interior regions
themes: Terrestrial vegetation response to climate change

10.1016/j.epsl.2006.03.009.title: Beyond methane: Towards a theory for the Paleocene–Eocene Thermal Maximum
10.1016/j.epsl.2006.03.009.short_summary: Study on Beyond methane: Towards a theory for the Paleocene–Eocene Thermal Maximum
key_findings: The Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM) requires explanatory mechanisms beyond methane release
A comprehensive theory for PETM warming needs to account for multiple carbon sources and feedback mechanisms
Carbon cycle dynamics during the PETM involved complex interactions beyond simple methane hydrate destabilization
10.1016/j.epsl.2006.03.009.methodology: The authors develop a theoretical framework examining the Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum by evaluating various carbon sources and climate feedback mechanisms beyond the commonly cited methane hypothesis.
limitations: No abstract available limits detailed extraction of specific methodologies employed
Limitations of specific models or datasets used cannot be determined from metadata alone
Scope of theoretical coverage cannot be precisely defined without full text
future_work: Integration of additional paleoclimate proxy data to test theoretical predictions
Refinement of carbon cycle models incorporating multiple carbon sources during the PETM
Investigation of feedback mechanisms linking carbon release to climate warming

10.1098/rsta.2010.0053.title: A Palaeogene perspective on climate sensitivity and methane hydrate instability
10.1098/rsta.2010.0053.venue: Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society A Mathematical Physical and Engineering Sciences
10.1098/rsta.2010.0053.short_summary: The Palaeocene–Eocene thermal maximum (PETM), a rapid global warming event and carbon-cycle perturbation of the early Palaeogene, provides a unique test of climate and carbon-cycle models as well as our understanding of sedimentary methane hydrate stability, albeit under conditions very different from the modern. The principal expression of the PETM in the geological record is a large and rapid negative excursion in the carbon isotopic composition of carbonates and organic matter from both marin
key_findings: The Palaeocene–Eocene thermal maximum (PETM) shows a temperature increase of approximately 5–6°C despite a carbon input of 4000–7000 PgC, suggesting climate sensitivities consistent with state-of-the-art models rather than anomalously high values

Evidence for methane hydrate dissociation during the PETM exists but the magnitude of carbon input required cannot be explained by hydrate release alone, indicating alternative major carbon sources were involved

Climate and carbon-cycle models can be effectively validated using the PETM as a natural experiment, as the geological record provides well-documented evidence of carbon isotopic changes and temperature anomalies from this transient event
10.1098/rsta.2010.0053.methodology: The study synthesizes carbon-cycle model estimates of carbon input magnitude and atmospheric CO2 perturbations during the PETM, reviews theoretical mass balance arguments and sedimentary evidence for methane hydrate dissociation, and compares the derived climate sensitivity estimates against proxy-based temperature anomalies and modern climate models.
limitations: Analysis relies on PETM conditions very different from modern climate
Uncertainty in precise quantification of atmospheric CO2 changes from proxy data
Difficulty in definitively separating methane hydrate contributions from other carbon sources based on available sedimentary evidence
future_work: Further refinement of carbon source attribution mechanisms beyond methane hydrates during the PETM
Improved paleoclimate proxy reconstructions to better constrain atmospheric CO2 and temperature anomalies
Development of more sophisticated coupled carbon-climate models to test climate sensitivity across different paleoclimatic scenarios

10.1038/nature10929.title: Past extreme warming events linked to massive carbon release from thawing permafrost
10.1038/nature10929.short_summary: Study on Past extreme warming events linked to massive carbon release from thawing permafrost
key_findings: Past extreme warming events are linked to massive carbon release from thawing permafrost
Permafrost thaw served as a significant carbon feedback mechanism during historical climate extremes
Paleoclimate evidence suggests permafrost carbon release contributed to rapid temperature increases in past warming episodes
10.1038/nature10929.methodology: The study likely analyzed paleoclimate records, sediment cores, and climate models to establish temporal correlations between warming events and permafrost thaw signatures, combined with carbon cycle modeling to quantify potential carbon releases.
limitations: No abstract available limits detailed understanding of methodology and scope
Study relies on indirect paleoclimate proxies which have inherent uncertainty
Difficulty in directly measuring historical permafrost carbon releases
future_work: Further investigation of permafrost carbon feedback mechanisms in modern climate change projections
Development of more precise paleoclimate proxies for detecting permafrost thaw
Integration of permafrost carbon dynamics into Earth system models for future warming scenarios

10.1016/j.gloplacha.2013.07.001.title: Up in smoke: A role for organic carbon feedbacks in Paleogene hyperthermals
10.1016/j.gloplacha.2013.07.001.short_summary: Study on Up in smoke: A role for organic carbon feedbacks in Paleogene hyperthermals
key_findings: Organic carbon feedbacks play a significant role in driving Paleogene hyperthermal events
The paper examines the mechanisms linking carbon cycling to rapid warming episodes during the Paleogene period
limitations: No abstract available limits detailed assessment of methodology and scope
Inference is based solely on title and metadata
future_work: Further investigation of organic carbon feedback mechanisms during hyperthermal events
Improved paleoclimate modeling incorporating carbon cycle dynamics

10.1029/2020pa003942.title: Synchronous Marine and Terrestrial Carbon Cycle Perturbation in the High Arctic During the PETM
10.1029/2020pa003942.short_summary: Abstract The Paleocene‐Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM; 56 Ma) is considered to be one of the best analogs for future climate change. The carbon isotope composition (δ 13 C) of n ‐alkanes derived from leaf waxes of terrestrial plants and marine algae can provide important insights into the carbon cycle perturbation during the PETM. Here, we present new organic geochemical data and compound‐specific δ 13 C data from sediments recovered from an early Cenozoic basin‐margin succession from Spitsbergen.
key_findings: Synchronous carbon isotope excursions (~-6.5‰ in marine algae and ~-5‰ in terrestrial plant waxes) were observed during the PETM peak in the high Arctic, indicating simultaneous perturbation of both marine and terrestrial carbon cycles
Carbon modeling suggests peak emission rates during the PETM were approximately 3 times faster than previously estimated using total organic carbon records
The coupled isotopic changes in both marine and terrestrial organic matter argue against significant fossil carbon weathering contributions to Arctic sedimentary organic carbon during the PETM
10.1029/2020pa003942.methodology: Compound-specific carbon isotope analysis (δ13C) of n-alkanes from leaf waxes and marine organic matter in sediment cores from Spitsbergen, combined with carbon cycle modeling to estimate emission rates and interpret carbon cycle dynamics.
limitations: Results may be affected by modest thermal effects (1-2‰) on δ13C values
Interpretation relies on an expanded PETM site from a single high Arctic basin-margin location
future_work: Further investigation of the 3x faster carbon emission rates and their implications for rapid climate change mechanisms
Broader geographic comparison with other PETM sites to test if synchronous marine-terrestrial perturbations are regionally or globally widespread

10.1029/2007pa001465.title: North American continental margin records of the Paleocene‐Eocene thermal maximum: Implications for global carbon and hydrological cycling
10.1029/2007pa001465.short_summary: The impacts of the Paleocene‐Eocene thermal maximum (PETM) (∼55 Ma), one of the most rapid and extreme warming events in Earth history, are well characterized in open marine and terrestrial environments but are less so on continental margins, a major carbon sink. Here, we present stable isotope, carbonate content, organic matter content, and C:N ratio records through the PETM from new outcrop sections in California and from cores previously drilled on the New Jersey margin. Foraminifer δ 18 O da
key_findings: North American continental margins warmed by 5-8°C during the PETM, similar to open ocean warming, but recorded a larger carbon isotope excursion (3.3-4.5‰) than documented in open ocean records

Sediment and carbon accumulation rates increased dramatically during the PETM on continental margins, with mass accumulation rates of organic and inorganic carbon increasing by an order of magnitude, contrary to open ocean patterns

Continental margins served as significant carbon sinks during the PETM due to weathering feedbacks and rising sea level, suggesting a greater negative feedback role for carbon burial on continental margins than previously recognized
10.1029/2007pa001465.methodology: The study analyzed stable isotope records (δ18O and δ13C), carbonate content, organic matter content, and C:N ratios from outcrop sections in California and previously drilled cores from the New Jersey margin through the PETM interval, comparing these records with open ocean data.
limitations: Study focused on North American continental margins; global applicability of the carbon sink role may be limited
Reliance on outcrop and existing core data rather than comprehensive new drilling program across all continental margins
future_work: Extend similar analysis to other continental margins globally to assess whether the North American pattern is representative
Further investigate the specific mechanisms of weathering feedbacks and their role in continental margin carbon cycling during hyperthermal events

10.1016/j.epsl.2014.10.027.title: Structure of the carbon isotope excursion in a high-resolution lacustrine Paleocene–Eocene Thermal Maximum record from central China
10.1016/j.epsl.2014.10.027.short_summary: Study on Structure of the carbon isotope excursion in a high-resolution lacustrine Paleocene–Eocene Thermal Maximum record from central China
key_findings: High-resolution lacustrine record from central China reveals detailed structure of carbon isotope excursion during PETM
Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum event is recorded in sedimentary sequences with isotopic signatures traceable in continental settings
10.1016/j.epsl.2014.10.027.methodology: The study likely analyzed sediment cores from a lacustrine (freshwater lake) deposit in central China using carbon isotope analysis to characterize the isotopic patterns during the PETM event.
limitations: No abstract available limits detailed understanding of specific methodological constraints and scope
Study is limited to a single lacustrine site in central China, which may not be representative of global patterns
future_work: Comparison with other PETM records from different paleoenvironments and geographical locations
Investigation of mechanisms driving carbon isotope variations during rapid climate warming events

10.1130/g35637.1.title: Extreme warming of tropical waters during the Paleocene–Eocene Thermal Maximum
10.1130/g35637.1.short_summary: The Paleocene–Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM), ca. 56 Ma, was a major global environmental perturbation attributed to a rapid rise in the concentration of greenhouse gases in the atmosphere. Geochemical records of tropical sea-surface temperatures (SSTs) from the PETM are rare and are typically affected by post-depositional diagenesis. To circumvent this issue, we have analyzed oxygen isotope ratios (δ18O) of single specimens of exceptionally well-preserved planktonic foraminifera from the PETM in
key_findings: Tropical sea-surface temperatures rose by more than 3°C during the PETM and may have exceeded 40°C, based on δ18O analysis of well-preserved planktonic foraminifera from Tanzania
Extreme environmental conditions during the PETM temporarily caused the absence of calcareous plankton in the studied record, indicating severe ecological disruption
Post-depositional diagenesis issues can be circumvented by analyzing single specimens of exceptionally well-preserved foraminifera to obtain accurate paleoclimate records
10.1130/g35637.1.methodology: The study analyzed oxygen isotope ratios (δ18O) of single specimens of exceptionally well-preserved planktonic foraminifera from PETM deposits in Tanzania, accounting for changes in seawater chemistry and pH to estimate tropical sea-surface temperatures during this climate event approximately 56 million years ago.
limitations: Calcareous plankton records are incomplete in the Tanzania PETM section, limiting the temporal resolution of the study
Geochemical records of tropical SSTs from the PETM are inherently rare and typically affected by post-depositional diagenesis
future_work: Expanding the geographic distribution of well-preserved PETM records to better constrain tropical SST estimates across different regions
Investigating the mechanisms of foraminiferal exclusion and ecosystem recovery during and after extreme warming events
