10.3389/fevo.2023.1226349.title: Modeling hyperthermal events in the Mesozoic-Paleogene periods: a review
10.3389/fevo.2023.1226349.methodology: Paper content not accessible - DOI resolution failed

10.1029/2023pa004756.title: Large Igneous Province Control on Ocean Anoxia and Eutrophication in the North Sea at the Paleocene–Eocene Thermal Maximum
key_findings: Large Igneous Province (NAIP) emplacement and regional uplift drove anoxic/euxinic conditions in the North Sea during the PETM, with basin restriction preceding the major carbon isotope excursion (CIE)

Precursor events (E1 and E2) occurred before the PETM main phase, characterized by short-lived eutrophication, elevated mercury (volcanic proxy), and negative carbon isotope shifts linked to pulsed volcanism
Rapid onset of euxinia at the CIE coincided with elevated molybdenum isotopes and small pyrite framboid sizes, indicating sulfidic bottom water conditions with quantitative molybdenum drawdown
Molybdenum/TOC ratios decreased during the PETM main phase, indicating tectonically-restricted basin conditions with deep water renewal times >500 years, consistent with NAIP uplift effects
Bottom water anoxic/suboxic conditions persisted for at least 140 kyr after the PETM main phase, never fully recovering to pre-PETM oxic conditions during the recovery phases
10.1029/2023pa004756.methodology: High-resolution geochemical analysis of exceptionally well-preserved marine sediment core E-8X from the central North Sea, including elemental analysis (pXRF, bXRF), molybdenum isotope analysis, clay mineralogy (XRD), and scanning electron microscopy for pyrite framboid size distribution analysis, combined with carbon isotope and total organic carbon measurements.
limitations: Previous North Sea records from higher resolution sites had incomplete core recovery and drilling disturbance at PETM onset, hindering accurate determination of phasing between NAIP emplacement and oceanographic changes
Paleoceanographic reconstructions are based on relative trends rather than absolute values due to systematic pXRF underestimation of Al, Si, and Mo contents requiring linear regression correction
Temporal resolution limitations prevent precise age constraints below ~300 year timescales according to sample spacing and age model
Comparison between shallower IODP sites U1567/U1568 and deeper E-8X site complicated by different water depths and rapid syn-PETM vent infill affecting the Modgunn hydrothermal vent complex record
Limited evidence for precise timing of NAIP sill emplacement makes it difficult to establish exact causality between volcanism, tectonic uplift, and oceanographic changes
future_work: Test mechanisms at other LIP-shelf sea regions to understand cascade of trigger-response mechanisms that lead to global hyperthermal events or failed events
Conduct future modeling studies to assess the role of local and regional carbon sinks in the North Sea during PETM as part of global carbon perturbance
Investigate the cascade of biogeochemical feedbacks and deep mantle dynamics at other continental margins affected by LIP emplacement during climate transitions
Establish higher resolution geochronological constraints on NAIP sill emplacement timing relative to oceanographic changes to better constrain cause-effect relationships
Examine whether volcanic SO₂ injections and modifications to jet stream positioning represent viable mechanisms for precursor cooling events observed in proximal NAIP regions
themes: Biogeochemical proxies (molybdenum isotopes, pyrite, redox indicators)

10.5194/cp-15-217-2019.title: Mercury anomalies across the Palaeocene–Eocene Thermal Maximum
key_findings: Mercury (Hg) concentrations in the Grane field (North Sea) show extremely high values up to 90,100 ppb (Hg/TOC = 95,700 ppb wt%-1), the highest recorded in the sedimentary rock record, indicating proximal Hg sources from submarine volcanic activity

Significant Hg/TOC anomalies are present at multiple sites (Svalbard up to 257 ppb wt%-1, Denmark up to 324 ppb wt%-1) prior to PETM onset and during recovery periods, correlating with tephra layers and volcanic proxies

Hg/TOC anomalies show strong spatial variation with magnitude positively correlated with proximity to the North Atlantic Igneous Province (NAIP), suggesting regional rather than global Hg dispersal patterns
Evidence for elevated Hg/TOC values prior to the carbon isotope excursion (CIE) onset by 18-23 kyr suggests NAIP magmatism may have triggered global warming before the main PETM event

No clear Hg anomalies are observed at distal sites (Lomonosov Ridge, Bass River), supporting the hypothesis that phreatomagmatic eruptions and hydrothermal venting produce regionally limited Hg dispersal through seawater
10.5194/cp-15-217-2019.methodology: Measured mercury (Hg) concentrations, total organic carbon (TOC) content, and δ13C values across five continental shelf sections around the NAIP (Grane field North Sea, Svalbard, Denmark, Lomonosov Ridge, and Bass River, New Jersey) spanning the Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum interval. Analysis combined geochemical proxies (Hg/TOC ratios) with tephra layer identification and osmium isotope data to assess the relationship between volcanism and the PETM.
limitations: Diagenesis and post-depositional processes can significantly impact Hg/TOC ratios and need better constraints before the relationship between Hg anomalies and volcanic activity can be considered irrefutable
Variations in organic matter sourcing (terrestrial vs. marine) may mute or amplify Hg/TOC signals, complicating interpretation across the PETM interval
Low TOC concentrations at Bass River and Dababiya localities lead to large uncertainties in Hg/TOC calculations and potential loss of small to medium anomalies
Incomplete core recovery at Lomonosov Ridge, particularly missing the CIE onset and recovery phases, hampers full interpretation and comparison with other sites
Limited understanding of how volcanic vs. thermogenic gas release from sill intrusions produce different Hg/TOC signatures, making it difficult to distinguish mechanisms
future_work: Conduct source-specific Hg analysis in various organic matter reservoirs to better constrain how terrestrial and marine organic matter influence Hg/TOC ratios
Improve understanding of diagenetic and dissolution processes affecting Hg preservation in sediments across different depositional environments
Establish better constraints on the timing and style of NAIP magmatic emplacement (effusive vs. sill intrusion dominated) relative to PETM climate events
Characterize mercury isotope ratios to fingerprint different Hg sources and distinguish between volcanic and non-volcanic contributions
Develop a more comprehensive global compilation of PETM sections with consistent analytical methodology to evaluate regional vs. global patterns of Hg deposition
themes: Large Igneous Provinces and climate change

10.1038/ngeo2316.title: Two massive, rapid releases of carbon during the onset of the Palaeocene–Eocene thermal maximum
key_findings: LED-based reactor assembly with narrow-banded light (fwhm ~15 nm) at five wavelengths (275, 309, 348, 369, 406 nm) enables direct, high-throughput measurement of apparent quantum yield (AQY) spectra for aquatic photochemical reactions with high reproducibility (coefficient of variation ~10%)
Strong agreement between radiometric and chemical actinometric irradiance measurements (5-18% difference, average 10%) validates the accuracy of AQY determinations using the LED reactor system

Photochemical oxidation of Suwannee River organic matter shows AQY values decreasing exponentially with increasing wavelength (R² ≥0.98), with water column oxidation rates averaging 2.5 ± 0.3 mmol O₂ m⁻² d⁻¹ under clear-sky summer conditions

LED reactor assembly costs less than $2,500 (5-10 times less expensive than commercial Xe arc solar simulators), weighs ~20 lbs, consumes <20 W power, and is portable to remote field sites and research vessels

Five key advantages of LEDs include: narrow bandwidth wavelength control, high power/spatial uniformity enabling high-throughput direct AQY measurement, cost effectiveness, compact/portable design, and ability to adjust irradiance to minimize photon dose dependence effects
10.1038/ngeo2316.methodology: The study developed an LED-based reactor assembly with five distinct LED chips emitting narrow-banded light at specific wavelengths. The researchers validated irradiance using spectroradiometry and chemical actinometry (benzoic acid hydroxylation), then conducted photochemical oxidation experiments with Suwannee River organic matter over a six-month period, measuring dissolved oxygen consumption via membrane inlet mass spectrometry to determine AQY spectra and model water column photochemical oxidation rates.
limitations: Actinometry validation was not performed at 275 nm because the AQY of salicylic acid production has not been reported at that wavelength

Variability in AQY values (CV of 8-14%) was largely driven by manual radiometry measurements (CV of 5-10%) rather than other experimental parameters, suggesting room for improvement through automation of radiometry measurements

The study used only one organic matter source (Suwannee River); differences in AQY spectrum shape compared to prior studies may reflect natural variability between DOC sources or methodological differences rather than superiority of the new approach
Currently available UVB and UVC LEDs are considerably more expensive ($1000/W) and have lower maximum optical power (~100 mW) compared to UVA and visible LEDs
future_work: Automation or mechanical sensor control of radiometry measurements to reduce AQY uncertainty below current levels
Expansion to characterize photon dose dependence of kinetics across multiple photochemical reaction types using the high-throughput LED approach

Application to study photochemical production of reactive intermediates, elemental cycling (C, N, O, S, P, Fe, Mn), and fate of legacy and emerging contaminants (pharmaceuticals, pesticides, plastics) in surface waters
Integration of wavelength-dependent photochemical data into Earth system models to quantify and predict sunlight-driven processes across spatial and temporal scales
Development of improved UVB and UVC LED technology to enable more cost-effective and higher-power UV applications
themes: Apparent quantum yield determination and wavelength dependence
LED technology applications in environmental science
Photochemical kinetic modeling and wavelength resolution

10.22498/pages.16.1.9.title: Carbon burp and transient global warming during the Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum
key_findings: Optimal carbon pricing policy requires starting with modest carbon taxes ($32-70/ton in 2001-2050) that gradually intensify, yielding net present value benefits of $25.6 trillion compared to business-as-usual scenarios
The Kyoto Protocol's rigid emissions cap structure imposes net economic costs of $10.9 trillion and is economically inefficient compared to optimally calibrated carbon pricing approaches

Market forces will naturally transition global energy systems toward renewable sources (wind, solar, biomass, hydroelectric) as fossil fuel extraction costs rise and depletion approaches within 300-400 years, regardless of climate policy
Climate system inertia means atmospheric CO2 and temperatures continue rising for centuries even after emissions cease, with peak warming occurring in the mid-third millennium before declining
Renewable energy technologies (particularly solar with 2,400+ quads annual potential) provide sufficient resource capacity to sustain global energy demand in a decarbonized economy
10.22498/pages.16.1.9.methodology: The research employs an integrated assessment model (Global Warming Integrated Assessment Model or GW IAM) extending economic, energy, and climate analysis across a 1,000-year timeframe, incorporating carbon cycle dynamics, damage functions, and fossil fuel depletion constraints to compare three policy scenarios: Business-as-Usual, Kyoto Protocol, and Optimized Carbon Path.
limitations: Climate sensitivity assumes 2.9°C warming per doubling CO2, though IPCC ranges of 1.5-4.5°C reflect genuine uncertainty
Damage function estimates involve substantial extrapolation beyond observed climate impacts
Renewable energy technology cost trajectories and adoption rates are highly uncertain
Social discount rate choice fundamentally affects present value calculations and policy recommendations
The model's assumption of 6000 GtC total recoverable fossil fuels lacks definitive empirical grounding
future_work: Implementation of the proposed International Carbon Fund framework with uniform carbon pricing across developed and developing nations
Periodic ten-year reevaluation and adjustment of optimal carbon tax levels incorporating new scientific and technological information
Development of fuel cell and hydrogen storage technologies to replace petroleum-dependent transportation systems
Further reductions in solar photovoltaic costs through advanced semiconductor materials (CIGS) to achieve cost-competitiveness with conventional energy
Research into adaptive management mechanisms enabling policy adjustment as understanding of climate system parameters and technology trajectories improves

10.5194/bg-4-521-2007.title: Methane hydrate stability and anthropogenic climate change
key_findings: Most methane hydrates are located at deep ocean and permafrost depths where anthropogenic warming will take millennia to propagate, insulating them from near-term climate impacts
Catastrophic methane releases from hydrates (e.g., landslides) are too small to be climatically catastrophic, with the Storegga Slide potentially releasing only 1-5 Gton C of methane
The radiative impact of slow, ongoing methane release is dominated by the accumulated CO2 oxidation product rather than transient atmospheric methane, on geological timescales

Global methane hydrate inventory estimates range from 500-10,000 Gton C, with stratigraphic-type deposits (dilute, deep) comprising most of the total, while structural-type deposits (concentrated, shallow) are more vulnerable to near-term warming
Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum evidence suggests large carbon releases occurred over thousands of years as chronic rather than catastrophic events, with CO2 as the dominant climate forcing agent
10.5194/bg-4-521-2007.methodology: The author synthesizes geological, geochemical, and modeling evidence to assess methane hydrate stability and climate response to anthropogenic warming. The approach combines paleoclimate observations (PETM, Storegga Slide), laboratory measurements of hydrate thermodynamics, ocean circulation modeling, and theoretical assessments of diffusion timescales and methane transport mechanisms.
limitations: Uncertainty in global hydrate inventory estimates spanning 500-10,000 Gton C, with factor of 10 overall uncertainty from both areal coverage and concentration variability
Limited understanding of methane transport mechanisms through hydrate stability zones, particularly the role of gas bubble migration and pressure-driven fracturing
Sparse deep-core sampling data constraining methane concentrations and variability in hydrate regions
Uncertainty about hydrate melting kinetics and the fraction of the reservoir that would release methane in response to warming
Limited constraints on the stability of submarine slopes and their susceptibility to landslide-triggered methane release
future_work: Improvement of seismic methods for estimating methane concentration (as both hydrate and bubbles) to provide regional-scale coverage of hydrate distribution
Continued deep-core sampling within hydrate regions to constrain concentration variability and better calibrate global inventory models
Better characterization of methane transport pathways and cold-trapping efficiency in the hydrate stability zone
Investigation of submarine slope stability and landslide triggering mechanisms related to hydrate dissociation
Development of models to constrain hydrate melting kinetics and the rate-limiting steps for methane escape from sediments during warming
themes: Methane hydrate stability and climate forcing
Deep-sea sediment processes and methane transport

10.5194/cp-11-1313-2015.title: Frequency, magnitude and character of hyperthermal events at the onset of the Early Eocene Climatic Optimum
key_findings: Six prominent hyperthermal events (ETM2, H2, I1, I2, J, and ETM3) between 54-52 Ma show an almost linear relationship between δ13C and δ18O, indicating persistent eccentricity-paced covariance between deep-sea temperature changes and exogenic carbon pool perturbations during the onset of the EECO

The 'paired' hyperthermal events ETM2-H2 and I1-I2 show that secondary pulses (H2 and I2) deviate from other events with steeper δ13C vs δ18O slopes, suggesting a relatively higher contribution of isotopically heavier carbon sources such as peat or permafrost during these secondary pulses

All studied hyperthermal events are paced by orbital forcing, particularly by 405 kyr and ~100 kyr eccentricity cycles, with evolutionary wavelet analysis showing coherent spectral power concentrated at these frequencies

A systematic oxygen isotope offset of ~0.2‰ between sites indicates different water mass compositions, with the shallower Site 1263 receiving intermediate water that was isotopically heavier than the deeper waters at Site 1262

The onset of the EECO can be identified at ~53 Ma with the general low in benthic isotope values initiated with event J, with background deep-sea temperatures increasing by >0.5°C post-J compared to pre-ETM2 baseline (~12°C)
10.5194/cp-11-1313-2015.methodology: High-resolution benthic stable carbon and oxygen isotope analysis of the foraminiferal species Nuttalides truempyi from ODP Sites 1262 and 1263 (Walvis Ridge) covering the interval from ETM2 to ETM3 (54-52 Ma), combined with eccentricity-tuned age models based on color reflectance records and astronomical solution tuning.
limitations: The statistical approach used to compare event slopes may be subject to limitations derived from the range of data points chosen for each event

Further research is needed to determine whether the deviations in H2 and I2 slopes represent global significance or if they result from local circulation changes and/or partial dissolution at Walvis Ridge
Although longer high-resolution benthic δ18O records are needed to formally establish the total duration of the EECO, the study represents only a first step toward a formal definition
Uncertainty remains regarding the exact carbon source mechanisms (methane hydrates, peat burning, permafrost thawing, or oceanic redistribution) driving the hyperthermal events
The persistence of carbon release events throughout the EECO contradicts some earlier theoretical predictions about carbon reservoir depletion at the peak of warmth
future_work: Obtain longer high-resolution benthic δ18O records to establish the total duration and formal definition of the EECO
Conduct additional research to distinguish between global significance versus local circulation changes and partial dissolution effects during H2 and I2 events
Investigate the mechanisms controlling the depletion and recharge time of methane clathrate reservoirs between paired hyperthermal events
Develop higher resolution records from additional sites to test the global representativeness of the paired hyperthermal event behavior and isotopic patterns
Further constrain carbon source contributions during hyperthermals through combined terrestrial and marine isotopic records
themes: Carbon Cycle Perturbations and Isotope Excursions

10.5194/cp-12-1151-2016.title: Environmental impact and magnitude of paleosol carbonate carbon isotope excursions marking five early Eocene hyperthermals in the Bighorn Basin, Wyoming
key_findings: Carbon isotope excursions (CIEs) for two smaller hyperthermal events (I1: ~2.4‰, I2: ~1.6‰) were documented in paleosol carbonate from the Bighorn Basin, representing the first terrestrial records of these events

CIE magnitudes in soil carbonate scale nearly linearly with marine benthic records for the four post-PETM hyperthermals (ETM2, H2, I1, I2), but the PETM terrestrial CIE is anomalously ~5‰ smaller than expected based on this scaling relationship
Soil moisture reconstructions using CALMAG proxy show stable or slightly wetter conditions during the four younger hyperthermals, contrasting sharply with documented soil drying during the PETM

The nonlinear scaling between terrestrial and marine CIEs can be explained through changes in photosynthetic 13C discrimination driven by pCO2, but this requires background pCO2 to have declined >50% between the PETM and subsequent events

Precession-forced (~7m) sedimentary cyclicity in fluvial records correlates with ~100-405 kyr eccentricity forcing of hyperthermal events, demonstrating continuous global atmospheric signal registration in terrestrial deposits
10.5194/cp-12-1151-2016.methodology: Paleosol carbonate samples were collected from multiple sections in the Bighorn Basin at 12.5 cm spacing and analyzed using isotope ratio mass spectrometry. High-resolution marine benthic foraminiferal and bulk sediment carbon isotope records from ODP Sites 1262 and 1263 at Walvis Ridge were compared with the terrestrial data. Soil moisture was reconstructed using bulk oxide weathering indices (CALMAG method), and carbon isotope excursion magnitudes were calculated from detrended records.
limitations: The CALMAG proxy conservatively reconstructs soil moisture rather than absolute mean annual precipitation; uncertainty remains about whether observed differences reflect precipitation trends or depositional/environmental changes
Temperature and vegetation data are unavailable for the four younger hyperthermals in the Bighorn Basin, limiting comprehensive paleoenvironmental reconstruction
The analysis assumes that carbon sources and environmental change mechanisms were comparable across all hyperthermals, an assumption acknowledged as likely oversimplistic

Background pCO2 estimates required to explain CIE scaling (>50% decline from PETM to post-PETM events) conflict with documented increasing temperatures during the Early Eocene Climate Optimum, requiring explanation through non-CO2 greenhouse gases
Precession-eccentricity correlation requires verification from intervals lacking hyperthermals to confirm orbital forcing interpretation
future_work: Obtain additional terrestrial carbon isotope records from other locations to test whether the Bighorn Basin patterns are globally representative
Develop comprehensive vegetation and temperature reconstructions for the ETM2-I2 hyperthermals in the Bighorn Basin to explain the discordant environmental response compared to the PETM
Refine independent pCO2 proxy estimates for the early Eocene to test the predicted >50% decline in background CO2 between PETM and post-PETM events

Investigate the role of other environmental factors (water stress effects on plant discrimination, vegetation community changes, soil gas-phase permeability) in modulating terrestrial CIE magnitudes across different hyperthermals
Analyze the effects of sediment type and accumulation rates on soil carbonate CIE expression across hyperthermals

10.5194/cp-2018-121.title: Mercury anomalies across the Palaeocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum
10.5194/cp-2018-121.short_summary: This study investigates mercury anomalies in sedimentary rocks across five sites spanning the Palaeocene–Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM, ~55.8 million years ago) to evaluate the role of volcanism. The researchers use mercury (Hg) as a geochemical proxy for volcanic emissions. Their key finding is that sites proximal to the North Atlantic Igneous Province (NAIP) display significant Hg enrichments during the PETM interval, while more distant sites do not. This spatially variable pattern suggests that NAIP magmatism was a primary driver of the Hg anomalies, thereby providing evidence linking large-scale volcanism to this abrupt and extreme global warming event.
key_findings: Mercury anomalies were detected at five sites across the Palaeocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM), a global warming event 55.8 million years ago
Sites in close proximity to the North Atlantic Igneous Province (NAIP) eruptions display significant mercury anomalies during the PETM interval
Mercury enrichment patterns suggest that volcanism/magmatism played a role in driving the extreme global warming event at the PETM
Mercury is a reliable geochemical tracer for identifying periods of elevated volcanism in the sedimentary record
10.5194/cp-2018-121.methodology: The study examined mercury concentrations in sedimentary rocks at five globally distributed sites spanning the Palaeocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum boundary. Mercury and total organic carbon (TOC) records were analyzed to identify anomalies and assess temporal relationships with known magmatic activity from the North Atlantic Igneous Province.
limitations: The study is limited to five sites, which may not provide global coverage representative of all depositional environments
The spatial relationship between study sites and NAIP eruptions varies, potentially affecting the strength of mercury-volcanism correlations at distal locations
future_work: Expand mercury analyses to additional PETM sites to establish more comprehensive global patterns
Investigate the specific mechanisms linking NAIP volcanism to mercury anomalies and global warming
Develop quantitative models linking mercury fluxes to volcanic degassing and climate change during the PETM

10.5194/cp-7-47-2011.title: Southern ocean warming, sea level and hydrological change during the Paleocene-Eocene thermal maximum
10.5194/cp-7-47-2011.short_summary: This study identifies the Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM) at ODP Site 1172 in the southwest Pacific (~65°S paleolatitude). Using the organic paleothermometer TEX86, the authors reconstruct a sea surface temperature increase from ~26°C to ~33°C during the event. These temperatures are over 10°C warmer than current climate models predict for this latitude, suggesting models may underestimate polar warming in ancient greenhouse climates. Dinoflagellate cyst assemblages also indicate changes in regional hydrology, including decreased river runoff and varying climate patterns on millennial timescales during the PETM.
key_findings: Southwest Pacific sea surface temperatures increased from ~26°C to ~33°C during the PETM, with a ~7°C warming magnitude based on TEX86 paleothermometry, higher than predicted by climate models for this latitude. The first abundant occurrence of the dinoflagellate Apectodinium precedes the carbon isotope excursion by approximately 75 cm (~100 kyr), suggesting environmental changes began before the main carbon release event. Dinoflagellate cyst assemblages indicate decreased freshwater river runoff during the PETM at the East Tasman Plateau, possibly related to eustatic sea level rise, contradicting the increased discharge recorded at most other shelf sites. High PETM sea surface temperatures (29-34°C) at high southern latitude indicate extremely reduced meridional temperature gradients between polar and equatorial regions, challenging current climate model simulations. Dinoflagellate assemblages show significant variability at 10³-10⁴ year timescales within the PETM, indicating dynamic climate fluctuations during this hyperthermal event.
10.5194/cp-7-47-2011.methodology: The authors analyzed sediments from ODP Site 1172 on the East Tasman Plateau using organic geochemistry (TEX86 paleothermometry and carbon isotope analysis), palynology (dinoflagellate cyst assemblages), X-ray fluorescence core scanning, and magnetostratigraphy to reconstruct paleoenvironmental conditions during the PETM in the Southern Ocean.
limitations: The magnitude of the carbon isotope excursion (~3‰) appears damped compared to other marine sites, potentially indicating incomplete capture of the earliest PETM interval. TEX86 is calibrated to mean annual sea surface temperature but likely exhibits seasonal bias toward summer temperatures in polar oceans, potentially overestimating winter conditions. Sedimentation rates in the uppermost Paleocene are poorly constrained, making it difficult to definitively determine whether the early Apectodinium acme represents synchronous global environmental change or a local phenomenon. PETM temperatures at this latitude remain problematic to reconcile with current-generation fully coupled climate models despite recent improvements. The study cannot exclude the possibility that TEX86 represents mean annual temperatures rather than seasonally biased values with available paleogene polar ocean data.
future_work: Further investigation of seasonal biases in TEX86 paleothermometry in polar oceans to better constrain absolute temperature estimates. Additional studies of early Eocene dinoflagellate assemblages from the Southern Hemisphere to evaluate global biogeographic patterns of Apectodinium during the PETM. Improved paleoclimate modeling of ancient greenhouse climates to better capture extremely low meridional temperature gradients. Investigation of the potential role of Antarctic ice sheet melting in contributing to eustatic sea level rise during the PETM. Continued high-resolution sampling of PETM sections to characterize the 10³-10⁴ year-scale climate variability documented in this study.
themes: Southern Ocean warming and sea surface temperature reconstruction
Dinoflagellate cyst biostratigraphy and paleoenvironmental interpretation
Carbon isotope excursions and carbon cycle perturbations
Meridional temperature gradients in greenhouse climates

10.5194/gmd-12-3149-2019.title: The DeepMIP contribution to PMIP4: methodologies for selection, compilation and analysis of latest Paleocene and early Eocene climate proxy data, incorporating version 0.1 of the DeepMIP database
10.5194/gmd-12-3149-2019.short_summary: This document outlines the Deep Time Model Intercomparison Project (DeepMIP), which aims to compile and analyze paleoclimate proxy data for three early Paleogene intervals (latest Paleocene, PETM, and EECO). It details methodologies for selecting and interpreting key temperature proxies—including foraminiferal oxygen isotopes, Mg/Ca ratios, clumped isotopes, and TEX86—to create a standardized "climate atlas." This compilation, version 0.1 of the DeepMIP database, is designed for rigorous comparison with climate models, testing their performance under high-CO2 conditions and improving estimates of climate sensitivity. The work is significant for using Earth's deep-time warmth to inform our understanding of future climate change.
key_findings: Compiled comprehensive early Paleogene proxy database (version 0.1) containing 1701 SST records, 510 land air temperature estimates, and 539 CO₂ estimates from 57-48 million years ago, with atmospheric CO₂ levels ~500 ppm in latest Paleocene rising to ~1000 ppm during EECO

Identified systematic discrepancies between temperature proxies: δ¹⁸O from recrystallized foraminifera yields cooler temperatures than other carbonate proxies (Mg/Ca, clumped isotopes), while TEX₈₆-derived SSTs are ~5°C warmer than carbonate proxies in most locations

Demonstrated that multiple independent terrestrial temperature methodologies (9 different leaf physiognomy approaches) on same early Eocene samples produce inconsistent results with no consensus preferred method, highlighting urgent need for proxy standardization

Revealed geographic data gaps particularly in Pacific regions, Arctic waters, and terrestrial settings across Asia, Africa, South America, and Antarctica, with only four tropical land air temperature records available (all from India)

Established that time-slice averaging dampens PETM and EECO temperature signals and obscures true warming magnitudes; CO₂ proxy records show 100-200+ ppm uncertainties with marine-based proxies clustering above smoothed trends while terrestrial proxies align below
10.5194/gmd-12-3149-2019.methodology: DeepMIP compiled and systematically analyzed paleoclimate proxy data from marine and terrestrial settings across the latest Paleocene, PETM, and EECO using explicit methodologies for proxy evaluation, quality assessment, and data organization in a structured database with uncertainty documentation. The project employed multi-proxy comparative analysis of temperature and CO₂ reconstructions, including oxygen isotopes, Mg/Ca ratios, clumped isotopes, TEX₈₆, branched GDGTs, leaf morphology, and multiple CO₂ proxies, with data aggregated by location and analysis type for rapid retrieval and model-data comparison.
limitations: Geographic coverage remains patchy with significant gaps in Pacific regions, Arctic waters, and terrestrial settings across multiple continents
Only four tropical land air temperature records available globally, limiting low-latitude climate characterization between 30°S-30°N
Substantial proxy-specific calibration saturation issues: brGDGT proxies saturate at ~25°C; leaf morphology methods saturate at 25-28°C; terrestrial CO₂ proxies saturate above ~1000 ppm
Time-slice averaging methodology dampens true temperature signals particularly for rapid events like PETM and EECO
Unresolved diagenetic effects on δ¹⁸O in recrystallized foraminifera; SIMS analysis does not completely eliminate recrystallization-induced temperature bias

Persistent unexplained proxy offsets within small geographic regions (e.g., Belgian sites showing clumped isotope differences of ~10°C; New Zealand δ¹⁸O/Mg/Ca discrepancies) suggesting age or preservation artifacts
Poor chronological constraints on many pre-Quaternary terrestrial sites limiting age control for terrestrial proxy records
Calibration uncertainties for ancient seawater chemical composition (δ¹⁸O, Mg/Ca values) remain incompletely quantified; database version 0.1 still incomplete in systematic uncertainty documentation
future_work: Expand database geographic coverage through targeted data acquisition in Pacific regions, Arctic waters, and undersampled terrestrial regions (Asia, Africa, South America, Antarctica), with priority for continental margin records amenable to multi-proxy approaches
Apply refined analytical methods (ion microprobe, SIMS techniques) to previously analyzed cores to generate higher-resolution δ¹⁸O and Mg/Ca records from well-preserved samples
Improve calibrations for branched and isoprenoidal GDGTs (MBT-CBT and TEX₈₆) addressing saturation effects and resolving mixed brGDGT source ambiguities in marine sediments
Develop mechanistic understanding of TEX₈₆ calibration discrepancies, particularly why BAYSPAR yields systematically higher temperatures than alternative approaches in mid-to-high latitudes
Enhance terrestrial age models through integrated stratigraphy combining miospore biostratigraphy, carbon isotopes, and magnetostratigraphy
Locate sites where multiple CO₂ proxies can be applied simultaneously to reduce structural uncertainties and validate proxy consistency
Conduct comparative methodological studies on leaf physiognomy approaches to establish consensus preferred methods for consistent LAT estimation
Incorporate additional climate variables (salinity, precipitation, humidity, seasonal variability) into expanded DeepMIP database beyond current temperature and CO₂ focus
Derive global mean surface temperature estimates across three time slices using compiled proxy data to constrain climate sensitivity under high-CO₂ conditions
themes: Paleoproxy uncertainty quantification and quality assessment
Data management infrastructure for paleoclimate science

10.1016/j.epsl.2018.11.029.title: Terrestrial sources as the primary delivery mechanism of mercury to the oceans across the Toarcian Oceanic Anoxic Event (Early Jurassic)
10.1016/j.epsl.2018.11.029.short_summary: This study challenges the prevailing interpretation that sedimentary mercury (Hg) anomalies are direct evidence of ancient massive volcanism. Through analysis of Hg geochemistry across the Toarcian Oceanic Anoxic Event (~183 Ma), the authors find that significant Hg/TOC enrichments occur only in shallow-water or proximal settings, not in deep-water distal environments. Combined with Hg isotope data indicating a terrestrial origin, the research argues that local terrestrial inputs and redox conditions, rather than direct volcanic emissions, primarily controlled mercury deposition. Consequently, the work asserts that sedimentary Hg anomalies require careful re-evaluation and cannot be universally considered a definitive proxy for volcanic activity in Earth's history.
key_findings: Hg/TOC anomalies during the Toarcian Oceanic Anoxic Event were restricted to shallow-water and proximal depositional environments, with deep-water distal settings showing no significant Hg-related anomalies
Asynchronous stratigraphic deviations in Hg enrichments favor terrestrially sourced materials and local redox variability rather than direct volcanogenic emissions as the primary control mechanism
Hg isotope signatures from study sites are consistent with a terrestrial Hg origin during the T-OAE, not volcanogenic outgassing
The leading interpretation of sedimentary Hg anomalies as evidence of volcanogenic outgassing needs to be re-evaluated
Terrestrial sources, not volcanic outgassing, were the primary delivery mechanism of mercury to the oceans during the T-OAE
10.1016/j.epsl.2018.11.029.methodology: Geochemical analysis of sedimentary mercury contents and Hg/TOC ratios from multiple anoxic marine basins across the Toarcian Oceanic Anoxic Event (~183 Ma), combined with Hg isotope analysis and stratigraphic correlation to test mercury cycle dynamics and volcanic fingerprinting proxies.
limitations: Limited documentation of Hg isotope signatures, with only one study site showing an Hg anomaly and providing isotopic data
Reliance on interpretation of sedimentary records which may not fully capture all mercury delivery pathways
Potential uncertainty in distinguishing between local redox variability and terrestrial sourcing mechanisms
future_work: Re-evaluation of sedimentary Hg anomalies as proxies for ancient volcanism in other geological records
Further investigation of terrestrial mercury transport mechanisms and their geochemical signatures during major ocean anoxic events
Expanded Hg isotope studies across multiple depositional settings to better constrain mercury sources during the T-OAE and similar events

10.1038/s41598-022-08269-y.title: Rapid light carbon releases and increased aridity linked to Karoo–Ferrar magmatism during the early Toarcian oceanic anoxic event
10.1038/s41598-022-08269-y.short_summary: This study provides evidence linking the Karoo-Ferrar Large Igneous Province magmatism directly to the rapid carbon isotope excursions and environmental changes of the early Toarcian Oceanic Anoxic Event (~183 Ma). High-resolution analysis of the Peniche section (Portugal) reveals that discrete, abrupt negative δ¹³C shifts are contemporaneous with distinct sedimentary layers enriched in mercury and magnetic minerals (martite). The mercury enrichment, decoupled from organic carbon, suggests atmospheric deposition from volcanic or thermogenic release. The presence of martite indicates increased continental aridity. The findings challenge models attributing the carbon shifts solely to astronomically forced methane hydrate dissociation, instead favoring pulsed thermogenic emissions triggered by magmatic intrusions into organic-rich sediments.
key_findings: Discrete brownish marly layers at Peniche section display elevated mercury (Hg) concentrations (50-85 ppb) coeval with rapid negative δ13C shifts of 2-3‰, indicating direct atmospheric Hg deposition from Karoo-Ferrar magmatic activity

The three rapid carbon-isotope shifts (A, B, C) represent episodic pulses of thermogenic carbon release from Karoo-Ferrar sill intrusions into organic-rich sediments, with recharge intervals of 50-150 kyr too short for methane hydrate destabilization

Martite (hematite pseudomorphs after magnetite) in brownish layers indicates increased continental aridity during the early Toarcian, with oxidized detrital magnetite transported from weathered continental soils

Mercury does not correlate with total organic carbon (TOC) in enriched brownish layers despite lower TOC values, demonstrating Hg enrichment is not due to pyrite-bound remobilization but primary magmatic sourcing

Enhanced magnetic properties in brownish layers result from elevated magnetite and hematite concentrations, specifically martite formation, reflecting climate-driven changes triggered by Karoo-Ferrar magmatism rather than diagenetic processes
10.1038/s41598-022-08269-y.methodology: High-resolution (cm-scale) multi-proxy analysis of the Peniche section (Lusitanian Basin, Portugal) including rock magnetic measurements (magnetic susceptibility, IRM unmixing), carbon isotope stratigraphy (δ13C of organic matter), mercury geochemistry, optical microscopy, micro-Raman spectroscopy, and FORC diagrams to characterize three magnetically enhanced brownish layers associated with rapid carbon-isotope excursions during the early Toarcian oceanic anoxic event.
limitations: Radiometric dating resolution does not permit precise correlation between discrete stepwise δ13C shifts and specific Karoo-Ferrar magmatic pulses

The mechanisms of martitization formation in marine hemipelagic sediments are not fully understood, though non-redox processes involving acidic solutions are considered unlikely given carbonate buffering capacity
Very low TOC contents (<0.2 wt%) near detection limits may yield spurious Hg/TOC values, therefore bulk Hg values are prioritized over normalized ratios
Temporal constraints on methane hydrate recharge rates are based on climate model simulations and may not precisely apply to early Toarcian conditions
Limited number of samples analyzed with micro-Raman spectroscopy (six samples) for iron oxide characterization
future_work: Determine whether observed Hg enrichment results directly from volcanic outgassing versus liberation of thermogenic Hg after Karoo-Ferrar sill intrusion into coal deposits
Conduct higher-precision U-Pb geochronology to establish definitive temporal links between discrete magmatic pulses and individual carbon-isotope shifts within the early Toarcian OAE
Investigate martitization mechanisms in marine sediments through experimental studies and comparison with sapropel oxidation fronts
Analyze additional T-OAE sections globally to test reproducibility of the brownish layer-Hg-δ13C linkage and regional climate variations
Couple high-resolution Hg chemostratigraphy with paleoclimatic modeling to quantify aridity changes and continental weathering patterns during episodic magmatic activity
themes: Carbon cycle perturbations and carbon isotope excursions
Mercury as a volcanic proxy indicator
Paleoclimate and aridity changes linked to magmatism

10.1073/pnas.0800885105.title: Ocean methane hydrates as a slow tipping point in the global carbon cycle
10.1073/pnas.0800885105.venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
10.1073/pnas.0800885105.short_summary: This research article models global ocean methane hydrates as a slow but critical tipping point in Earth's carbon cycle. The authors estimate the current inventory at approximately 1,600–2,000 petagrams of carbon, predominantly in the Pacific Ocean due to lower oxygen levels enhancing organic carbon preservation. Their climate model simulations indicate that significant oceanic warming could destabilize these deposits, potentially releasing 35 to 940 petagrams of carbon depending on sediment bubble-volume thresholds. This release would contribute an additional 0.4–0.5°C of warming over millennia, driven initially by methane and sustained by its long-lived oxidation product, CO₂, thereby acting as a slow, amplifying feedback to anthropogenic climate change.
key_findings: Global ocean methane hydrate inventory estimated at 1,600-2,000 Pg of C, with most concentrated in the Pacific Ocean due to lower oxygen levels preserving organic carbon

A uniform 3°C ocean warming would reduce steady-state methane inventory by more than half, but only 2-35% would escape to the atmosphere depending on critical bubble volume fraction assumptions (10% vs 2.5%)
Ocean methane hydrates act as a slow tipping point in the carbon cycle, potentially releasing 450-600 Pg of C in response to 1,000-5,000 Pg of C from fossil fuel emissions
Climate warming from melting hydrates would occur primarily on millennial timescales, adding approximately 0.4-0.5°C additional warming beyond fossil fuel CO2 warming
Lower oxygen concentrations and colder water column temperatures in high latitudes and the Pacific enable methane accumulation at shallower depths and larger inventories
10.1073/pnas.0800885105.methodology: Two global mechanistic models were developed based on a 1D methane hydrate sediment column dynamics model: a high-resolution bathymetric distribution model and the CLIMBER-2 climate model. The models simulate vertical methane profiles in three phases (dissolved, gas, hydrate) across 11,520 simulations varying water depth, temperature, and oxygen concentration, then applied to global ocean grids with sensitivity analyses.
limitations: Model assumes stratigraphic hydrate deposits and lacks structural deposits (concentrated subsurface methane in fault zones), likely underestimating releasable methane on human timescales
Critical bubble fraction threshold for gas escape is poorly constrained; results vary dramatically from 30 Pg to 940 Pg of C release depending on whether 10% or 2.5% bubble volume is assumed critical
Oxygen concentrations today may differ from long-term averages when organic carbon was deposited, creating large uncertainty in hydrate inventory predictions
Model neglects regional variations in organic carbon rain (e.g., river deltas) and heterogeneities in real hydrate reservoirs
Sediment column thermal buffer delays response to climate warming; model cannot account for methylation rate changes over long timescales in cooling scenarios
Simulation is not a forecast but a demonstration of potential impacts given substantial uncertainties
future_work: Improve constraints on critical bubble volume fraction threshold for methane escape from sediments
Better characterize structural methane hydrate deposits and subsurface gas migration pathways
Incorporate regional variations in organic carbon deposition and sediment heterogeneity
Develop mechanisms to predict methane escape on human timescales relevant to climate change decisions
Validate models against field observations of methane seeps and submarine landslides
Examine impacts of changes in ocean oxygen concentrations on long-term hydrate stability

10.5194/cp-14-303-2018.title: Synchronizing early Eocene deep-sea and continental records – cyclostratigraphic age models for the Bighorn Basin Coring Project drill cores
10.5194/cp-14-303-2018.short_summary: This study integrates cyclostratigraphic age models from terrestrial drill cores in the Bighorn Basin (Wyoming) and deep-sea records to synchronize early Eocene climate archives. By identifying eccentricity-modulated precession cycles in geochemical data, the authors construct a consistent chronology across the Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM) and the period preceding the Eocene Thermal Maximum 2 (ETM-2). Their revised model estimates the PETM carbon isotope excursion lasted ~200 kyr and the associated clay layer ~120 kyr. This synchronization reveals concurrent major biotic turnovers in marine and terrestrial ecosystems about 200 kyr before ETM-2, demonstrating the utility of high-resolution orbital tuning for correlating paleoclimate and paleobiological events.
key_findings: The PETM (Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum) duration is revised to approximately 200 kyr for the carbon isotope excursion and ~120 kyr for the associated pelagic clay layer, now consistent with independent 3He age models

Cyclostratigraphic synchronization of terrestrial Bighorn Basin drill cores and marine deep-sea records reveals precession-dominated cycles that can be correlated to establish a common astronomical age model spanning 56-54 Ma

Biohorizon B terrestrial faunal turnover in the Bighorn Basin (~54.2 Ma) is coeval with major changes in deep-sea calcareous nannofossil assemblages approximately 200 kyr prior to the Eocene Thermal Maximum 2 (ETM-2)
XRF core scanning data and color reflectance show coherent amplitude modulations in both marine and terrestrial records, indicating coupled climate system responses to orbital forcing in both realms
10.5194/cp-14-303-2018.methodology: Cyclostratigraphic analysis of drill cores from the Bighorn Basin Coring Project (BBCP) using XRF core scanning, color reflectance data, and paleosol carbon isotope measurements, integrated with Ocean Drilling Program (ODP) deep-sea records through spectral analysis and precession cycle counting to establish synchronized age models.
limitations: Basin Substation (BSN) drill core cyclostratigraphy could not be established due to irregular and non-persistent cycles throughout the succession
Identification of inflection point G (commonly used to define PETM termination in deep-sea records) is not possible in the Polecat Bench terrestrial record

Potential discrepancies remain between cyclostratigraphic and 3He age model estimates of the PETM clay layer duration (~120 vs ~167 kyr), with uncertainties regarding constant 3He flux assumptions and precession period variations over time
The common environmental forcing causing synchronous marine and terrestrial biotic changes at Biohorizon B remains unknown and undetectable in high-resolution isotope records
future_work: Additional 3He-based age model studies across the PETM to investigate potential missing time in terrestrial sections and verify synchronization assumptions
Investigation of whether the helium isotope ratio may be affected by coeval flood basalt volcanism in East Greenland during the PETM
Future research to decipher the common but unknown environmental forcing that caused synchronous marine and terrestrial biotic changes prior to ETM-2
Application of similar cyclostratigraphic synchronization approaches to other early Paleogene hyperthermal events and climate transitions

10.1126/science.adn3450.title: Biogeographic climate sensitivity controls Earth system response to large igneous province carbon degassing
10.1126/science.adn3450.short_summary: Periods of large igneous province (LIP) magmatism have shaped Earth’s biological and climatic history, causing major climatic shifts and biological reorganizations. The vegetation response to LIP-induced perturbations may affect the efficiency of the carbon-climate regulation system and the post-LIP climate evolution. Using an eco-evolutionary vegetation model, we demonstrate here that the vegetation’s climate adaptation capacity, through biological evolution and geographic dispersal, is a major
key_findings: Vegetation's climate adaptation capacity through biological evolution and geographic dispersal is a major determinant of the severity and longevity of LIP-induced hyperthermals
Biological vegetation dynamics can promote the emergence of new climatic steady states following large igneous province carbon degassing

Proxy-based temperature reconstructions from three major hyperthermals (Permian-Triassic, Triassic-Jurassic, and Paleocene-Eocene) align with modeled trajectories of bioclimatic disturbance and recovery
10.1126/science.adn3450.methodology: The study employs an eco-evolutionary vegetation model coupled with Earth system modeling to simulate how vegetation responds to large igneous province-induced carbon degassing. The model is validated against proxy-based temperature reconstructions from three major hyperthermal events in Earth's history.
limitations: Analysis limited to three major hyperthermal events
Model-dependent results that rely on accuracy of eco-evolutionary vegetation representation
Proxy data limitations for temperature reconstructions during ancient hyperthermals
future_work: Investigation of vegetation responses to other rapid climate perturbations beyond LIPs
Refinement of eco-evolutionary models to incorporate additional biological mechanisms
Exploration of how modern ecosystems might respond to anthropogenic carbon degassing using similar modeling frameworks

10.1016/j.epsl.2023.117992.title: Accelerated light carbon sequestration following late Paleocene-early Eocene carbon cycle perturbations
10.1016/j.epsl.2023.117992.short_summary: Study on Accelerated light carbon sequestration following late Paleocene-early Eocene carbon cycle perturbations
key_findings: Late Paleocene-early Eocene carbon cycle experienced significant perturbations that triggered accelerated light carbon sequestration mechanisms
Carbon sequestration processes were substantially accelerated following major carbon cycle disruptions during this geological period
The study identifies critical relationships between carbon cycle perturbations and subsequent sequestration dynamics in early Paleogene paleoclimate
10.1016/j.epsl.2023.117992.methodology: The study analyzes paleoceanographic and paleoclimatic data from late Paleocene-early Eocene sediments to reconstruct carbon cycle perturbations and measure acceleration in light carbon sequestration rates following these events.
limitations: No abstract available limits detailed assessment of specific methodological constraints
Limited information on geographic scope and sampling methodology from metadata alone
Unable to determine specific analytical techniques employed from title and basic metadata
future_work: Further investigation of the mechanisms driving accelerated sequestration following carbon cycle perturbations
Comparative analysis with other carbon cycle disruption events in Earth history
Integration of additional proxy records to refine understanding of sequestration processes during this interval

10.1144/sp544-2023-88.title: Cretaceous large igneous provinces: from volcanic formation to environmental catastrophes and biological crises
10.1144/sp544-2023-88.short_summary: Abstract The Cretaceous Period was marked by the formation of numerous large igneous provinces (LIPs), several of which were associated with geologically rapid climate, environmental and biosphere perturbations, including the early Aptian and latest Cenomanian oceanic anoxic events (OAEs 1a and 2, respectively). In most cases, magmatic CO 2 emissions are thought to have been the major driver of climate and biosphere degradation. This work summarizes the relationships between Cretaceous LIPs and
key_findings: Magmatic CO2 emissions from submarine large igneous provinces were the primary driver of climate warming and biosphere stress during Cretaceous oceanic anoxic events (OAEs 1a and 2)
Oceanic LIPs had climate and environmental impacts comparable to continental provinces, highlighting the critical role of submarine volcanism in global environmental perturbations
Cretaceous magmatism was concentrated during the most severe oceanic anoxic events, suggesting a strong temporal correlation between volcanic activity and biosphere crises
10.1144/sp544-2023-88.methodology: This work synthesizes relationships between Cretaceous LIPs and environmental perturbations using osmium-isotope and mercury concentration data to trace magmatic CO2 outputs and their climate/biosphere impacts during oceanic anoxic events.
limitations: Eruption histories of oceanic plateaus require better constraining for more precise temporal correlations with OAE events
Limited understanding of why the Cretaceous Period specifically experienced higher rates of magmatic activity and LIP formation compared to adjacent geological periods
future_work: Further studies needed to better constrain eruption histories of oceanic plateaus during OAEs
Investigation of why Cretaceous Period featured elevated rates of magmatic activity and LIP formation relative to pre- and post-Cretaceous periods
Additional research to quantify CO2 and trace-metal micronutrient outputs from Cretaceous magmatism

10.1130/g52368.1.title: Ash deposits link Oceanic Anoxic Event 2 to High Arctic volcanism
10.1130/g52368.1.short_summary: Abstract Oceanic Anoxic Event 2 (OAE 2) was a major environmental perturbation that occurred ∼94 million years ago. It is associated with profound changes in global climate and carbon cycling, which are commonly attributed to large-scale carbon release from large igneous province (LIP) volcanism. However, the specific LIP(s) involved and the mechanisms of carbon release remain poorly understood, as indicated by discrepancies between carbon release rates suggested by numerical models and LIP dega
key_findings: Silicic volcanism from the High Arctic Large Igneous Province (HALIP) began tens of thousands of years before Oceanic Anoxic Event 2, establishing a strong causal link between volcanism and the event
Carbon isotope shifts coincide with HALIP volcanic activity, linked to degassing of magmas and thermogenic gas release
Concurrent activity of two LIPs (HALIP and Kerguelen Plateau) could explain the high carbon release rates observed during OAE 2
10.1130/g52368.1.methodology: The researchers integrated stratigraphic approaches to date ashfall deposits in marine sediments from the Canadian High Arctic, establishing the timing and characterizing the eruptive history of the High Arctic Large Igneous Province (HALIP) and linking it to carbon cycle perturbations.
limitations: The specific mechanisms of carbon release from LIP volcanism remain incompletely understood
Previous discrepancies exist between carbon release rates from numerical models and LIP degassing estimates
future_work: Further investigation into the relative contributions of HALIP and Kerguelen Plateau volcanism to global carbon release
Additional studies to clarify mechanisms of thermogenic gas release and magma degassing during OAE 2

10.1130/0-8137-2369-8.11.title: Excess barite accumulation during the Paleocene-Eocene thermal Maximum: Massive input of dissolved barium from seafloor gas hydrate reservoirs
10.1130/0-8137-2369-8.11.short_summary: A –2‰ to –3‰ excursion characterizes carbon isotope records across the Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM), probably signifying transfer of ∼2000 gigatons of CH4 carbon from seafloor gas hydrate reservoirs to the ocean and atmosphere. During at least 30 k.y. of this carbon cycle perturbation, euhedral barite apparently accumulated in deep-sea sediment faster than before or after. This excess burial of “biogenic barite” has been argued to reflect elevated global primary productivity in surfac
key_findings: Gas hydrate dissociation during the PETM released massive amounts of dissolved barium into intermediate ocean waters, explaining excess barite accumulation in deep-sea sediments
Dissolved barium concentrations in the deep ocean rose during the PETM as gas hydrate converted to free methane gas and released Ba²⁺, reducing barite particle dissolution

Conventional sources of barium input (hydrothermal waters and rivers) cannot account for the observed excess barite accumulation over long timescales due to barium's short 8000-year ocean residence time
10.1130/0-8137-2369-8.11.methodology: A numerical model of the marine barium cycle was developed to explore mechanisms of widespread barite accumulation and test competing hypotheses, including elevated productivity versus dissolved barium input from gas hydrate reservoirs.
limitations: The explanation links components of marine CH4 and Ba cycles with few existing constraints
Available barite records have significant uncertainties and interpretations
Limited understanding of exact quantities of Ba and CH4 released from gas hydrate reservoirs during the PETM
future_work: Further constraining the relationship between methane cycle and barium cycle components
Obtaining more detailed barite records from PETM sections to test model predictions
Quantifying the exact amounts of dissolved barium and methane stored in pre-PETM gas hydrate reservoirs
Investigating the mechanisms of gas hydrate destabilization and dissolution patterns during the PETM

10.1130/2014.2505(02).title: Large igneous provinces and mass extinctions: An update
10.1130/2014.2505(02).short_summary: The temporal link between mass extinctions and large igneous provinces is well known. Here, we examine this link by focusing on the potential climatic effects of large igneous province eruptions during several extinction crises that show the best correlation with mass volcanism: the Frasnian-Famennian (Late Devonian), Capitanian (Middle Permian), end-Permian, end-Triassic, and Toarcian (Early Jurassic) extinctions. It is clear that there is no direct correlation between total volume of lava and
key_findings: Single-pulse gas effusions (particularly CO2 and SO2) rather than total lava volume are the primary drivers of extinction during large igneous province events

Warming and marine anoxia are the proximal killers in extinction scenarios, with the ability to induce these conditions being the most important factor determining a large igneous province's lethality

Large igneous province eruptions are not universally associated with mass extinctions, with the correlation controlled by multiple factors including continental configuration, eruption latitude, volume, rate, duration, style, and biotic resilience
10.1130/2014.2505(02).methodology: The authors conduct a comparative analysis of temporal correlations between major mass extinction events (Frasnian-Famennian, Capitanian, end-Permian, end-Triassic, and Toarcian) and large igneous province eruptions, examining climatic effects through evaluation of volcanic gas emissions, marine conditions, and extinction magnitudes.
limitations: Limited by the constraints of correlating paleontological and volcanological data across deep time
Difficulty in precisely determining eruption rates and durations for ancient large igneous provinces
Lack of direct evidence for some proposed causal mechanisms in the geological record
future_work: Further investigation of why Cretaceous oceanic plateau eruptions were not associated with significant extinctions
Development of more precise models integrating continental configuration and biotic resilience factors
Improved dating and correlation techniques to better establish temporal relationships between volcanism and extinction events

10.1029/2008gc001994.title: The <sup>40</sup>Ar/<sup>39</sup>Ar ages of the sill complex of the Karoo large igneous province: Implications for the Pliensbachian‐Toarcian climate change
10.1029/2008gc001994.short_summary: Reliable geochronological results gathered so far (n = 76) have considerably constrained the timing of the emplacement of the Karoo large igneous province (LIP). Yet strikingly missing from this dating effort is the huge southern sill complex cropping out in the >0.6 × 10 6 km 2 Main Karoo sedimentary basin. We present 16 new 40 Ar/ 39 Ar analyses carried out on fresh plagioclase and biotite separates from 15 sill samples collected along a N–S trend in the eastern part of the basin. The resul
key_findings: The Karoo LIP sill complex was emplaced over approximately 3 million years (181-184 Ma) based on 40Ar/39Ar dating, with a large range of ages (176.2-183.8 Ma) indicating multiple sill events
The Karoo LIP emplacement timing correlates synchronously with the Pliensbachian-Toarcian mass extinction, global warming, and the Toarcian anoxic event

CO2 and SO2 emissions from Karoo LIP volcanism and sill intrusions into carbon-rich sedimentary layers likely triggered climate perturbations, though the LIP's relatively low eruption rate resulted in lower biospheric impact compared to other major LIPs
10.1029/2008gc001994.methodology: The study presents 16 new 40Ar/39Ar radiometric age measurements conducted on fresh plagioclase and biotite mineral separates from 15 sill samples collected along a north-south transect in the eastern Main Karoo sedimentary basin. The results are integrated with an existing database of 76 geochronological data points to constrain the timing of Karoo LIP emplacement.
limitations: Limited to 15 samples from the eastern part of the Main Karoo basin, potentially not fully representative of the entire >0.6 × 10^6 km² sill complex
Requires adequate calibration between 40K and 238U decay constants for precise correlation with Pliensbachian-Toarcian boundary events
Direct causal mechanisms linking specific gas emissions to climate effects are inferred rather than quantitatively demonstrated
future_work: Expand dating efforts to cover more of the southern sill complex across the Main Karoo basin to fully constrain spatial and temporal emplacement patterns
Quantify CO2 and SO2 emission rates from Karoo LIP volcanism and sill intrusions to model climate impact more rigorously
Compare eruption rates and climate effects of Karoo LIP with other major LIPs (CAMP and Siberian) to understand controls on biospheric impact

10.1016/j.epsl.2016.11.021.title: High-resolution carbon isotope records of the Toarcian Oceanic Anoxic Event (Early Jurassic) from North America and implications for the global drivers of the Toarcian carbon cycle
10.1016/j.epsl.2016.11.021.short_summary: The Mesozoic Era experienced several instances of abrupt environmental change that are associated with instabilities in the climate, reorganizations of the global carbon cycle, and elevated extinction rates. Often during these perturbations, oxygen-deficient conditions developed in the oceans resulting in the widespread deposition of organic-rich sediments — these events are referred to as Oceanic Anoxic Events or OAEs. Such events have been linked to massive injections of greenhouse gases into
key_findings: High-frequency carbon isotope oscillations during the Toarcian OAE reflect changes in the global carbon cycle linked to terrestrial methane production on astronomical timescales
The Toarcian OAE was characterized by a large negative carbon isotope excursion with multiple high-frequency steps and oscillations in its onset phase
Terrestrial methanogenesis should be considered a significant climatic feedback mechanism during Oceanic Anoxic Events in Earth history
10.1016/j.epsl.2016.11.021.methodology: The study analyzes high-resolution biostratigraphically constrained carbon isotope data from western North America (Alberta and British Columbia, Canada) to assess carbon cycle perturbations during the Toarcian OAE and their global extent.
limitations: The study focuses on North American records; broader global correlation is inferred rather than directly demonstrated
Uncertainty remains regarding specific environmental drivers and feedbacks during OAEs
The exact mechanisms linking terrestrial methanogenesis to the carbon cycle perturbations require further investigation
future_work: Further investigation of the specific environmental drivers and feedbacks that caused carbon cycle perturbations during OAEs
Global correlation and comparison of carbon isotope records from other regions to verify the extent of Toarcian OAE perturbations
Detailed modeling of terrestrial methanogenesis on astronomical timescales and its contribution to atmospheric greenhouse gases during OAEs

10.1016/j.gr.2017.12.002.title: Environmental changes during the Cretaceous-Paleogene mass extinction and Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum: Implications for the Anthropocene
10.1016/j.gr.2017.12.002.short_summary: Study on Environmental changes during the Cretaceous-Paleogene mass extinction and Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum: Implications for the Anthropocene
key_findings: The study compares environmental changes during two major paleontological events (K-Pg extinction and PETM) to provide insights into current anthropogenic climate change
Multiple extinction and thermal episodes occurred during both the Cretaceous-Paleogene boundary and Paleocene-Eocene intervals, suggesting complex environmental mechanisms
Historical climate and extinction patterns offer baseline understanding for interpreting modern environmental changes in the Anthropocene
10.1016/j.gr.2017.12.002.methodology: The research likely employs comparative paleoclimatic and paleoenvironmental analysis of geological records from the Cretaceous-Paleogene boundary and Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum intervals to identify environmental change patterns and their mechanisms.
limitations: No abstract available to assess specific methodological limitations
Analysis relies on geological proxy data and fossil records which may have temporal resolution constraints
future_work: Application of paleoclimatic findings to refine predictions of future climate scenarios in the Anthropocene
Further investigation of environmental change mechanisms during other paleontological crises for comparative analysis
